detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a framework for evaluating and optimizing image compression architectures by directly aligning performance with human visual perception, addressing the limitation of traditional metrics like PSNR that fail to capture perceptual quality. To enable differentiable optimization and meaningful comparison, we employ two human vision-inspired metrics: Multi-Scale Structural Similarity (MS-SSIM) and Peak Signal to Noise Ratio - Human Visual System (PSNR-HVS), computed across model iterations on reconstructed images. Model rankings are derived from the area under the rate-distortion curve (AUC) across these metrics. Experimental results on the Kodak Photo CD dataset reveal that no single architecture consistently outperforms others under both metrics, highlighting the need for perceptually aligned evaluation. Our analysis of eight RNN-based architectures, including GRU and LSTM variants with residual and associative mechanisms, demonstrates that while some models achieve higher AUC on one metric, their performance diverges on the other. Notably, Residual GRU (One Shot) achieves the highest PSNR-HVS AUC on the High Entropy dataset, while LSTM (One Shot) leads on MS-SSIM AUC. These findings underscore the importance of perceptual metrics in guiding compression model design and suggest that optimal architectures may be context-dependent.
detailed_results.reference_summary: This paper presents a set of full-resolution lossy image compression methods based on neural networks. Each of the architectures we describe can provide variable compression rates during deployment without requiring retraining of the network: each network need only be trained once. All of our architectures consist of a recurrent neural network (RNN)-based encoder and decoder, a binarizer, and a neural network for entropy coding. We compare RNN types (LSTM, associative LSTM) and introduce a new hybrid of GRU and ResNet. We also study “one-shot” versus additive reconstruction architectures and introduce a new scaled-additive framework. We compare to previous work, showing improvements of 4.3%–8.8% AUC (area under the rate-distortion curve), depending on the perceptual metric used. As far as we know, this is the first neural network architecture that is able to outperform JPEG at image compression across most bitrates on the rate-distortion curve on the Kodak dataset images, with and without the aid of entropy coding.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose DenseNet, a novel deep learning architecture designed to address the vanishing gradient and information loss challenges inherent in very deep convolutional networks. Unlike ResNets, which use additive identity mappings to preserve information, DenseNet explicitly differentiates between feature-map additions and preservation by connecting each layer to all preceding layers, enabling direct feature reuse and reducing redundancy. Each layer contributes only a small number of feature-maps (e.g., 12) to a growing “collective knowledge” tensor, while preserving all prior feature-maps unchanged. This design allows the final classifier to leverage the full cumulative representation without requiring explicit skip connections. Training proceeds via standard backpropagation through this dense connectivity, with no stochastic layer dropping or topology modification. Empirical results across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that DenseNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly fewer parameters than ResNets and comparable or superior performance to other deep architectures. Theoretically, DenseNet’s design ensures information propagation through all layers, mitigating gradient vanishing and enabling deeper networks without performance degradation. This architecture offers a scalable, parameter-efficient solution for deep feature learning with broad applicability in computer vision and beyond.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose DenseNet, a novel deep neural network architecture designed to mitigate the vanishing gradient problem and enhance feature reuse by explicitly differentiating between information preservation and addition. Unlike ResNets, which use additive identity mappings, DenseNet connects every layer to every subsequent layer, enabling the direct propagation of feature-maps and allowing each layer to access the collective knowledge of all preceding layers. This design, combined with narrow layers (e.g., 12 filters) and transition layers that reduce dimensionality, ensures that only a small set of new features is added per layer while preserving existing ones. Trained end-to-end, DenseNet achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN, demonstrating significant improvements over ResNets and other deep architectures, particularly at higher depths with fewer parameters. Theoretical and empirical results confirm that DenseNet’s connectivity pattern enables more efficient gradient flow and feature reuse, leading to superior generalization and reduced overfitting. This approach provides a scalable framework for building deeper networks without the need for stochastic depth or complex layer bypasses.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Recent work has shown that convolutional networks can be substantially deeper, more accurate, and efficient to train if they contain shorter connections between layers close to the input and those close to the output. In this paper, we embrace this observation and introduce the Dense Convolutional Network (DenseNet), which connects each layer to every other layer in a feed-forward fashion. Whereas traditional convolutional networks with L layers have L connections—one between each layer and its subsequent layer—our network has \(\frac{L(L+1)}{2}\) direct connections. For each layer, the feature-maps of all preceding layers are used as inputs, and its own feature-maps are used as inputs into all subsequent layers. DenseNets have several compelling advantages: they alleviate the vanishing-gradient problem, strengthen feature propagation, encourage feature reuse, and substantially reduce the number of parameters. We evaluate our proposed architecture on four highly competitive object recognition benchmark tasks (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and ImageNet). DenseNets obtain significant improvements over the state-of-the-art on most of them, whilst requiring less computation to achieve high performance. Code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/liuzhuang13/DenseNet.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel framework for learning node representations in graph-structured data that explicitly decouples structural and feature-based modeling, addressing the limitation of traditional graph neural networks (GCNs) that rely on the assumption that connected nodes share labels—an assumption that may not reflect real-world graph semantics. Our method integrates a supervised loss \( \mathcal{L}_0 \) with a graph Laplacian regularizer \( \Delta = D - A \) to enforce smoothness while preserving discriminative feature encoding via a differentiable function \( f(\cdot) \). We introduce a weighting factor \( \lambda \) to balance these components, enabling flexible adaptation to heterogeneous graph data. Theoretically, we demonstrate that even untrained GCNs with random weights can act as powerful feature extractors, drawing parallels with the 1-dim Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL-1) algorithm, where node representations evolve through iterative neighborhood aggregation. Empirical results on standard benchmarks show improved generalization and robustness under varying graph structures. Our approach offers a principled way to incorporate structural priors without sacrificing expressive capacity, with implications for semi-supervised learning and graph representation learning in complex, real-world networks.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) framework that learns node representations by aggregating features from neighbors via a differentiable, neural network-based function, while incorporating supervised loss on labeled nodes and leveraging the graph structure through the unnormalized Laplacian. Our method addresses the limitation of traditional graph-based label propagation by relaxing the assumption that graph edges must encode node similarity, thereby enabling modeling of diverse edge semantics. The GCN architecture, inspired by the Weisfeiler-Lehman algorithm, iteratively refines node embeddings through message passing, where each layer aggregates neighborhood features via a hash-based coloring mechanism. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets—CiteSeer, Cora, Pubmed, and NELL—demonstrate state-of-the-art performance: achieving 70.3%, 81.5%, 79.0%, and 66.0% accuracy respectively, with significantly reduced training times compared to prior methods. These results confirm the GCN’s efficacy as a powerful, trainable feature extractor and classifier for graph-structured data, offering a scalable and theoretically grounded approach for node representation learning.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present a scalable approach for semi-supervised learning on graph-structured data that is based on an efficient variant of convolutional neural networks which operate directly on graphs. We motivate the choice of our convolutional architecture via a localized first-order approximation of spectral graph convolutions. Our model scales linearly in the number of graph edges and learns hidden layer representations that encode both local graph structure and features of nodes. In a number of experiments on citation networks and on a knowledge graph dataset we demonstrate that our approach outperforms related methods by a significant margin.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a unified framework for automatic image-to-image translation, addressing the challenge of mapping one pixel-based representation of a scene to another—such as RGB images to edge maps or semantic label fields—without task-specific architectures. Leveraging convolutional neural networks and conditional adversarial training, our method employs a shared architecture and objective function, trained solely on task-specific datasets, enabling generalization across diverse translation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate consistent performance across multiple domains, including photo-to-edge, sketch-to-photo, and semantic segmentation, with quality metrics matching or exceeding state-of-the-art specialized methods. Theoretical analysis confirms the framework’s capacity to learn invariant pixel-to-pixel mappings under varying output spaces. This approach not only reduces engineering overhead by eliminating custom models per task but also advances the field by revealing that a single neural architecture, when conditioned appropriately, can effectively handle the full spectrum of image-to-image translation problems, thereby unifying disparate research efforts under a common theoretical and practical paradigm.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a unified framework for automatic image-to-image translation, addressing the challenge of mapping diverse pixel representations of scenes—such as semantic labels, edge maps, or grayscale images—into corresponding output images using a common architecture. Our method leverages conditional adversarial networks (cGANs) trained with a combination of L1 pixel reconstruction loss and adversarial loss, enabling a single model to generalize across multiple translation tasks without task-specific modifications. We demonstrate this approach across six distinct image translation problems, including label-to-scene, aerial-to-map, and edge-to-photo synthesis, achieving state-of-the-art results. Quantitative evaluation shows that our L1+cGAN model significantly outperforms alternatives like GAN and cGAN, with per-pixel accuracy reaching 0.66 and class IOU of 0.17, surpassing ground truth metrics in several cases. This work establishes a general-purpose, end-to-end trainable solution for pixel-to-pixel mapping, reducing the need for specialized architectures and highlighting the efficacy of adversarial learning in complex visual translation tasks.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We investigate conditional adversarial networks as a general-purpose solution to image-to-image translation problems. These networks not only learn the mapping from input image to output image, but also learn a loss function to train this mapping. This makes it possible to apply the same generic approach to problems that traditionally would require very different loss formulations. We demonstrate that this approach is effective at synthesizing photos from label maps, reconstructing objects from edge maps, and colorizing images, among other tasks. Indeed, since the release of the pix2pix software associated with this paper, a large number of internet users (many of them artists) have posted their own experiments with our system, further demonstrating its wide applicability and ease of adoption without the need for parameter tweaking. As a community, we no longer hand-engineer our mapping functions, and this work suggests we can achieve reasonable results without hand-engineering our loss functions either.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a framework to mitigate exposure bias in deep generative models for text, a critical limitation of standard teacher-forcing training where models are conditioned solely on ground-truth inputs, leading to mismatched behavior at test time. To address this, we introduce a gradual feedback mechanism that progressively incorporates the model’s own predictions during training, controlled by a schedule parameter p that increases over time, enabling the model to learn robust generation dynamics without relying on ground-truth supervision. This approach, inspired by prior work on teacher-forcing and professor-forcing, is applied within an encoder-decoder architecture using a CNN encoder and LSTM decoder, trained end-to-end via back-propagation on the MSCOCO dataset. Experimental results demonstrate significant improvements in test-time generation quality, measured by BLEU and CIDEr scores, compared to baseline teacher-forcing methods. Theoretically, the gradual exposure ensures the model’s internal dynamics converge toward those of a self-consistent generator, reducing error accumulation during inference. This work advances the state-of-the-art in captioning and provides a generalizable strategy for training generative models under exposure bias.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures for image captioning to mitigate exposure bias inherent in teacher-forcing training, where models are conditioned on ground-truth inputs during training but must generate outputs autonomously at test time. Our framework employs a dual-path training strategy: a primary path using teacher-forcing and a secondary path that gradually incorporates model-generated outputs with increasing probability p, alongside a novel SCST (Self-Critical Sequence Training) objective that optimizes caption quality via reinforcement learning. The model architecture integrates a CNN encoder and an LSTM decoder, trained end-to-end using back-propagation. Experimental results on the MSCOCO dataset demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms baseline teacher-forcing and state-of-the-art methods, achieving a CIDEr score of 106.3, a 5.4-point improvement over the previous best. This advancement not only enhances caption quality but also provides a generalizable framework for mitigating exposure bias in sequence generation tasks, improving robustness and performance under real-world conditions.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Recently it has been shown that policy-gradient methods for reinforcement learning can be utilized to train deep end-to-end systems directly on non-differentiable metrics for the task at hand. In this paper we consider the problem of optimizing image captioning systems using reinforcement learning, and show that by carefully optimizing our systems using the test metrics of the MSCOCO task, significant gains in performance can be realized. Our systems are built using a new optimization approach that we call self-critical sequence training (SCST). SCST is a form of the popular REINFORCE algorithm that, rather than estimating a “baseline” to normalize the rewards and reduce variance, utilizes the output of its own test-time inference algorithm to normalize the rewards it experiences. Using this approach, estimating the reward signal (as actor-critic methods must do) and estimating normalization (as REINFORCE algorithms typically do) is avoided, while at the same time harmonizing the model with respect to its test-time inference procedure. Empirically we find that directly optimizing the CIDEr metric with SCST and greedy decoding at test-time is highly effective. Our results on the MSCOCO evaluation sever establish a new state-of-the-art on the task, improving the best result in terms of CIDEr from 104.9 to 114.7.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose YOLO9000, a real-time object detection framework capable of identifying over 9000 object categories by jointly optimizing detection and classification through shared feature representations. Addressing the critical gap between detection’s limited dataset scale and classification’s vast, well-labeled corpora, YOLO9000 leverages joint training on ImageNet and COCO, using WordTree to unify heterogeneous data sources. The architecture employs Intersection-over-Union (IOU) for bounding box regression and backpropagates objectness loss under this metric, enabling unified learning of localization and classification. YOLOv2, its predecessor, is also introduced as a state-of-the-art, scalable detection system adaptable to varying image sizes for speed-accuracy tradeoffs. Experimental results demonstrate YOLO9000’s efficacy in real-time detection across diverse datasets, significantly narrowing the dataset size disparity between detection and classification tasks. This work establishes a foundational approach for scalable, high-throughput detection and paves the way for extending similar techniques to weakly supervised segmentation and other vision tasks.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose YOLO9000, a real-time object detection framework designed to scale detection capabilities to match the breadth of object classification, addressing the critical gap between dataset size and object diversity in detection. YOLO9000 achieves this by jointly optimizing detection and classification through a unified neural network architecture, leveraging joint training on the large-scale ImageNet classification dataset and the COCO detection dataset. This approach enables the model to learn both object localization from COCO and fine-grained classification from ImageNet, thereby expanding its capacity to detect over 9000 object categories in real-time. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLO9000 achieves state-of-the-art performance, with an average Intersection over Union (IOU) of 67.2% using cluster-based anchor box generation, outperforming prior methods including Anchor Boxes and Cluster SSE. The framework’s efficiency and scalability represent a significant step toward closing the dataset disparity between detection and classification tasks, while its architecture also supports future extensions to weakly supervised segmentation.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We introduce YOLO9000, a state-of-the-art, real-time object detection system that can detect over 9000 object categories. First we propose various improvements to the YOLO detection method, both novel and drawn from prior work. The improved model, YOLOv2, is state-of-the-art on standard detection tasks like PASCAL VOC and COCO. Using a novel, multi-scale training method the same YOLOv2 model can run at varying sizes, offering an easy tradeoff between speed and accuracy. At 67 FPS, YOLOv2 gets 76.8 mAP on VOC 2007. At 40 FPS, YOLOv2 gets 78.6 mAP, outperforming state-of-the-art methods like Faster R-CNN with ResNet and SSD while still running significantly faster. Finally we propose a method to jointly train on object detection and classification. Using this method we train YOLO9000 simultaneously on the COCO detection dataset and the ImageNet classification dataset. Our joint training allows YOLO9000 to predict detections for object classes that don’t have labelled detection data. We validate our approach on the ImageNet detection task. YOLO9000 gets 19.7 mAP on the ImageNet detection validation set despite only having detection data for 44 of the 200 classes. On the 156 classes not in COCO, YOLO9000 gets 16.0 mAP. But YOLO can detect more than just 200 classes; it predicts detections for more than 9000 different object categories. And it still runs in real-time.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel framework to address the fundamental limitation of maximum likelihood generative modeling, where additive noise—essential for theoretical tractability—degrades sample quality and is empirically ill-suited to real data distributions. This issue arises because standard parametric models fail to yield valid densities on low-dimensional manifolds, rendering KL divergence undefined or infinite, thereby invalidating the likelihood-based optimization paradigm. To resolve this, we introduce a Wasserstein GAN-based approach that directly minimizes Wasserstein distance without relying on noise regularization, enabling training on distributions with non-smooth or manifold-supported supports. Our method constructs a discriminator that estimates the Wasserstein distance between real and generated data, allowing for gradient-based optimization without requiring density assumptions. Empirical results on image datasets demonstrate superior sample fidelity and stability compared to noise-augmented models, with Wasserstein distance consistently lower than alternatives. Theoretically, our framework guarantees convergence under mild conditions and avoids the pathological noise scaling observed in classical models. This work redefines generative modeling by decoupling likelihood optimization from noise injection, offering a principled alternative for manifold-structured data.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Wasserstein GAN (WGAN), a framework designed to address the fundamental instability and mode collapse issues inherent in traditional GANs by optimizing the Wasserstein distance instead of the adversarial objective. The method introduces a critic network trained to estimate the Wasserstein distance between real data and generated samples, with its weights clipped to a small range to ensure Lipschitz continuity, thereby stabilizing training. The generator is then updated to minimize the critic’s output on generated samples. Experimental results demonstrate that WGAN achieves superior sample quality and convergence stability compared to standard GANs, as evidenced by lower Wasserstein distance and Jensen-Shannon divergence metrics across multiple datasets. Theoretical analysis confirms that the Wasserstein distance provides a meaningful, continuous measure of distribution divergence, even when the supports of the real and generated distributions are disjoint, resolving a key limitation of maximum likelihood approaches that rely on noise regularization. This framework offers a principled, theoretically grounded alternative to adversarial training, with broad applicability in generative modeling.
detailed_results.reference_summary: The problem this paper is concerned with is that of unsupervised learning. Mainly, what does it mean to learn a probability distribution? The classical answer to this is to learn a probability density. This is often done by defining a parametric family of densities \( (P_\theta)_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d} \) and finding the one that maximized the likelihood on our data: if we have real data examples \( \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^m \), we would solve the problem

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a unified encoder-decoder framework for two core semantic role labeling tasks—link prediction and entity classification—leveraging relational graph structure to infer missing knowledge. Recognizing that neighborhood context in knowledge graphs encodes implicit entity types and relational triples, we design an R-GCN-based encoder to produce latent entity representations, followed by a tensor factorization decoder that reconstructs missing triples. Training is performed end-to-end via a contrastive loss that aligns encoded representations with ground-truth triples and types. Evaluated on large-scale KBs including Yago and DBPedia, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on both tasks, outperforming baselines by 5–12% in mean reciprocal rank and F1-score. Theoretical analysis confirms that the encoder captures structural and semantic inductive biases, enabling generalization to unseen entities and relations. This work advances scalable, context-aware knowledge graph completion, with direct implications for question answering, information retrieval, and semantic parsing systems relying on incomplete relational data.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a unified encoder-decoder framework for two fundamental semantic role labeling tasks—link prediction and entity classification—leveraging the relational structure of knowledge graphs. Addressing the inherent incompleteness of large-scale knowledge bases like Yago or Wikidata, our model employs an R-GCN encoder to generate latent feature representations of entities, followed by a tensor factorization decoder that exploits these representations to infer missing triples and entity types. Training is performed end-to-end on heterogeneous graph data, where entities are labeled with types and relations are encoded with their semantic roles. Experimental results across diverse datasets (AIFB, MUTAG, BGS, AM) demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in both tasks, with metrics showing significant gains over prior methods. The framework’s theoretical grounding in graph neural networks and tensor decomposition enables scalable inference while preserving relational semantics. This approach not only advances knowledge graph completion but also provides a principled method for structured entity annotation, with broad implications for question answering and information retrieval systems.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Knowledge bases organize and store factual knowledge, enabling a multitude of applications including question answering (Yao and Van Durme 2014; Bao et al. 2014; Seyler, Yahya, and Berberich 2015; Hixon, Clark, and Hajishirzi 2015; Bordes et al. 2015; Dong et al. 2015) and information retrieval (Kotov and Zhai 2012; Dalton, Dietz, and Allan 2014; Xiong and Callan 2015b; 2015a). Even the largest knowledge bases (e.g. DBPedia, Wikidata or Yago), despite enormous effort invested in their maintenance, are incomplete, and the lack of coverage harms downstream applications. Predicting missing information in knowledge bases is the main focus of statistical relational learning (SRL).

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a cycle-consistent adversarial network (CycleGAN) for unpaired image-to-image translation, enabling direct style transfer between two unordered image domains without requiring paired training data. Addressing the challenge of translating images across domains where correspondence is unavailable, our framework leverages adversarial learning to generate realistic outputs while preserving structural integrity through a cycle consistency loss that enforces invertibility. The architecture consists of two generators and two discriminators, trained end-to-end via a minimax game with cycle consistency constraints, allowing translation in both directions. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets—including Monet paintings to Flickr photos, zebras to horses, and summer to winter Yosemite scenes—demonstrate high fidelity and stylistic accuracy, validated by perceptual metrics and human evaluation. Our method achieves state-of-the-art results in style transfer without paired supervision, enabling applications such as artistic rendering, photo enhancement, and domain adaptation. This work establishes a foundational framework for unsupervised cross-domain image translation, with broad implications for computer vision, creative AI, and multimedia processing.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks (CycleGAN) to address the challenge of unpaired image-to-image translation, where corresponding images between two domains are not available. Our framework leverages adversarial learning to generate realistic translations while enforcing cycle consistency to preserve structural integrity, eliminating the need for paired training data. The core architecture consists of two generators and two discriminators, trained end-to-end via a cycle-consistency loss that ensures translating an image from domain X to Y and back to X recovers the original. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets—including Monet paintings to landscape photos, zebras to horses, and summer to winter scenes—demonstrate state-of-the-art performance. Quantitative metrics, such as human evaluation (% Turkers labeling outputs as real), show our method significantly outperforms prior approaches like CoGAN, BiGAN, and SimGAN. This enables novel applications in style transfer, domain adaptation, and artistic rendering, demonstrating that meaningful translation can be learned from unordered image collections alone.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Image-to-image translation is a class of vision and graphics problems where the goal is to learn the mapping between an input image and an output image using a training set of aligned image pairs. However, for many tasks, paired training data will not be available. We present an approach for learning to translate an image from a source domain X to a target domain Y in the absence of paired examples. Our goal is to learn a mapping G : X → Y such that the distribution of images from G(X) is indistinguishable from the distribution Y using an adversarial loss. Because this mapping is highly under-constrained, we couple it with an inverse mapping F : Y → X and introduce a cycle consistency loss to enforce F(G(X)) ≈ X (and vice versa). Qualitative results are presented on several tasks where paired training data does not exist, including collection style transfer, object transfiguration, season transfer, photo enhancement, etc. Quantitative comparisons against several prior methods demonstrate the superiority of our approach.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose Neural Message Passing (NMP), a graph neural network architecture designed to model molecular systems with invariance to graph isomorphism, leveraging atomic connectivity and feature propagation across message-passing layers. Unlike prior methods reliant on handcrafted features, NMP learns representations directly from atomic coordinates and bond types, enabling scalable prediction of quantum chemical properties without explicit feature engineering. Trained on large-scale datasets of molecules and their computed properties—such as energy and dipole moments—NMP achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on benchmark tasks, often outperforming traditional machine learning models and even matching or surpassing expensive quantum mechanical calculations. Theoretical analysis confirms its inductive bias aligns with molecular symmetries, enhancing generalization. This framework demonstrates that message-passing neural networks can effectively capture complex chemical interactions, offering a scalable, interpretable, and generalizable approach to automate high-throughput chemical discovery and materials design.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a message passing neural network (MPNN) framework specifically designed to predict quantum chemical properties of molecules, addressing the challenge of modeling complex, high-dimensional molecular data with computational efficiency. The model operates on graph representations of molecules, where atoms are nodes and bonds are edges, and employs message passing to aggregate local atomic features—such as atom type, hybridization, and hydrogen count—into global molecular representations. This architecture is invariant to graph isomorphism, aligning with the symmetries of atomic systems. We train the MPNN using a dataset of molecules with ground truth properties derived from computationally expensive density functional theory (DFT) calculations, enabling the model to learn accurate predictions at a speed of ~10⁻² seconds compared to ~10³ seconds for DFT. Experimental results demonstrate that the MPNN achieves high accuracy on tasks such as predicting molecular energy and dipole moment, outperforming baseline models. This work establishes a scalable, theoretically grounded approach for automating chemical property prediction, with broad implications for accelerating drug discovery and materials science.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Supervised learning on molecules has incredible potential to be useful in chemistry, drug discovery, and materials science. Luckily, several promising and closely related neural network models invariant to molecular symmetries have already been described in the literature. These models learn a message passing algorithm and aggregation procedure to compute a function of their entire input graph. At this point, the next step is to find a particularly effective variant of this general approach and apply it to chemical prediction benchmarks until we either solve them or reach the limits of the approach. In this paper, we reformulate existing models into a single common framework we call Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) and explore additional novel variations within this framework. Using MPNNs we demonstrate state of the art results on an important molecular property prediction benchmark; these results are strong enough that we believe future work should focus on datasets with larger molecules or more accurate ground truth labels.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose MobileNets, a class of efficient convolutional neural networks built from depthwise separable convolutions—comprising depthwise convolutions that apply a single filter per input channel, followed by 1×1 pointwise convolutions that linearly combine outputs—combined with batch normalization and ReLU nonlinearities after each convolutional layer. This architecture reduces computational complexity while maintaining performance, particularly through the use of strided depthwise convolutions for downsampling and average pooling for spatial reduction before fully connected layers. MobileNets consist of 28 layers, with depthwise and pointwise convolutions counted as separate layers, enabling significant efficiency gains over standard convolutions. Experimental results demonstrate that MobileNets achieve competitive accuracy on benchmark datasets while requiring substantially fewer parameters and FLOPs, outperforming prior small networks such as SqueezeNet and Inception models in efficiency. The design enables scalable deployment on resource-constrained devices, advancing the state of the art in mobile and embedded vision systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose MobileNets, a class of efficient convolutional neural networks designed for mobile and embedded vision tasks, addressing the critical challenge of deploying high-performance models on resource-constrained devices. The core architecture leverages depthwise separable convolutions, decomposing standard convolutions into depthwise convolutions (applying a single filter per input channel) followed by pointwise convolutions (1×1 convolutions for channel combination), significantly reducing computational cost while preserving representational capacity. Each convolutional layer is paired with batch normalization and ReLU nonlinearity, and downsampling is achieved via strided depthwise convolutions. The network is structured with 28 layers, culminating in average pooling and a fully connected classifier. Experimental results demonstrate MobileNets achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on benchmarks like ImageNet while requiring orders of magnitude fewer parameters and FLOPs compared to existing models, enabling real-time inference on mobile platforms for diverse applications including object detection, face attributes, fine-grained classification, and landmark recognition. This architecture provides a scalable, efficient foundation for deploying deep learning on edge devices.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present a class of efficient models called MobileNets for mobile and embedded vision applications. MobileNets are based on a streamlined architecture that uses depthwise separable convolutions to build lightweight deep neural networks. We introduce two simple global hyperparameters that efficiently trade off between latency and accuracy. These hyper-parameters allow the model builder to choose the right-sized model for their application based on the constraints of the problem. We present extensive experiments on resource and accuracy tradeoffs and show strong performance compared to other popular models on ImageNet classification. We then demonstrate the effectiveness of MobileNets across a wide range of applications and use cases including object detection, fine-grain classification, face attributes, and large-scale geo-localization.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose GraphSAGE, a general inductive framework for learning node embeddings in large graphs that naturally generalizes to unseen nodes, overcoming the transductive limitation of prior methods. Unlike existing approaches that require all nodes during training, GraphSAGE learns a differentiable function that generates embeddings by sampling and aggregating features from a node’s local neighborhood, leveraging both structural and node feature information. The framework is agnostic to feature availability, supporting graphs with text attributes (e.g., citation or biological data) or purely structural features (e.g., degree). We demonstrate that this neighborhood-aware aggregation effectively captures local roles and global positions, enabling accurate downstream prediction tasks. Empirical results on citation and social networks show GraphSAGE achieves state-of-the-art performance on node classification and link prediction, while maintaining computational efficiency. Theoretically, the method provides a principled way to approximate high-dimensional node representations through local structural and feature patterns. GraphSAGE thus offers a scalable, generalizable foundation for inductive learning on graphs, applicable across domains from recommendation to bioinformatics.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose GraphSAGE, a general inductive framework for learning node embeddings in large graphs that naturally generalizes to unseen nodes, addressing the limitation of existing transductive methods. GraphSAGE learns a differentiable function that generates embeddings by sampling and aggregating features from a node’s local neighborhood across multiple propagation layers, enabling efficient inference on new data. The algorithm leverages both node features (e.g., text attributes) and structural features (e.g., degrees) through a depth-K forward propagation process involving weight matrices, differentiable aggregator functions, and a non-linearity. Empirical results demonstrate GraphSAGE’s efficiency and effectiveness: training is significantly faster than competing inductive methods, and inference scales linearly with neighborhood size. On citation and biological graphs, GraphSAGE achieves state-of-the-art performance, with Micro F1 scores exceeding 0.83 and runtime scaling sublinearly with neighborhood sample size, validating its ability to capture both local role and global position through neighborhood aggregation. This framework provides a scalable, generalizable approach for node representation learning in dynamic and large-scale graph settings.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Low-dimensional vector embeddings of nodes in large graphs1 have proved extremely useful as feature inputs for a wide variety of prediction and graph analysis tasks [5, 11, 28, 35, 36]. The basic idea behind node embedding approaches is to use dimensionality reduction techniques to distill the high-dimensional information about a node’s graph neighborhood into a dense vector embedding. These node embeddings can then be fed to downstream machine learning systems and aid in tasks such as node classification, clustering, and link prediction [11, 28, 35].

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose the Transformer, a novel neural network architecture that entirely replaces recurrence and convolutions in sequence modeling with self-attention mechanisms, addressing the limitations of sequential processing in long-range dependency modeling. The core framework consists of stacked encoder-decoder layers, each employing multi-head self-attention and position-wise feed-forward networks, with positional encoding injected to preserve sequential order. Training is conducted via the Adam optimizer on large-scale parallel corpora, achieving state-of-the-art performance on machine translation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate superior translation quality and efficiency over RNN and CNN baselines, with consistent gains across multiple language pairs and model sizes. Theoretical analysis confirms the model’s ability to capture global dependencies without sequential computation, enabling parallelization and scalability. This work establishes self-attention as a foundational component for sequence modeling, influencing subsequent architectures in NLP, vision, and speech processing.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose the Transformer, a novel neural network architecture that entirely replaces recurrence and convolution with self-attention mechanisms, addressing the sequential processing bottleneck and limited parallelization of traditional RNNs and CNNs. The core innovation is the self-attention mechanism, which computes weighted sums of values based on the relevance of all input positions, enabling global context modeling without sequential dependencies. This is implemented via scaled dot-product attention, followed by multi-head attention to capture diverse sub-patterns, and a feed-forward network for local feature transformation. The model is trained end-to-end using standard optimization techniques like Adam, with positional encoding added to inject sequence order information. Experiments demonstrate that the Transformer achieves state-of-the-art performance on machine translation tasks, significantly outperforming recurrent and convolutional baselines while enabling near-complete parallelization. Theoretical analysis confirms its superior computational efficiency, with complexity per layer and maximum path length advantages over alternatives, making it a foundational architecture for scalable sequence modeling.
detailed_results.reference_summary: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train. Our model achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, improving over the existing best results, including ensembles, by over 2 BLEU. On the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, our model establishes a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8 after training for 3.5 days on eight GPUs, a small fraction of the training costs of the best models from the literature. We show that the Transformer generalizes well to other tasks by applying it successfully to English constituency parsing both with large and limited training data.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose ShuffleNet, a novel CNN architecture specifically designed to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy within extremely constrained computational budgets—tens to hundreds of MFLOPs—targeting deployment on mobile platforms such as smartphones and drones. Unlike prior work that focuses on pruning or compressing existing architectures, ShuffleNet introduces a lightweight, efficient design principle centered on channel shuffling and grouped convolutions to reduce redundancy while preserving representational capacity. Trained end-to-end on ImageNet and MS COCO, ShuffleNet demonstrates superior performance compared to MobileNet, achieving a 7.8% absolute reduction in top-1 error at 40 MFLOPs. The architecture leverages depthwise separable convolutions and channel-wise shuffling to maintain accuracy without proportional increases in FLOPs, enabling high-quality vision tasks on resource-limited devices. Our controlled experiments validate the effectiveness of these design choices across diverse benchmarks, establishing ShuffleNet as a foundational model for efficient, high-accuracy neural networks in embedded vision systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose ShuffleNet, a novel CNN architecture specifically designed to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy within extremely constrained computational budgets—tens to hundreds of MFLOPs—targeting deployment on mobile platforms like smartphones and drones. Addressing the gap left by existing compression techniques that modify pre-existing architectures, ShuffleNet introduces a lightweight, group-convolution-based structure with a novel channel shuffle operation that reorders feature channels to maximize information flow without additional computation. This design enables efficient feature extraction while maintaining high accuracy. Evaluated on ImageNet and MS COCO, ShuffleNet significantly outperforms MobileNet at 40 MFLOPs, achieving a 7.8% absolute reduction in top-1 error. The architecture’s complexity scales predictably with group count, maintaining consistent parameter and FLOP counts across configurations. These results demonstrate that a purpose-built, efficient architecture can surpass legacy models in constrained settings, offering a critical design paradigm for deploying high-performance vision models on resource-limited embedded systems.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We introduce an extremely computation-efficient CNN architecture named ShuffleNet, which is designed specially for mobile devices with very limited computing power (e.g., 10-150 MFLOPs). The new architecture utilizes two new operations, pointwise group convolution and channel shuffle, to greatly reduce computation cost while maintaining accuracy. Experiments on ImageNet classification and MS COCO object detection demonstrate the superior performance of ShuffleNet over other structures, e.g. lower top-1 error (absolute 7.8%) than recent MobileNet [12] on ImageNet classification task, under the computation budget of 40 MFLOPs. On an ARM-based mobile device, ShuffleNet achieves ~13× actual speedup over AlexNet while maintaining comparable accuracy.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a regularization framework for recurrent neural networks that operates directly on the recurrent weight matrices rather than hidden activations, addressing the limitations of traditional methods like dropout and batch normalization that disrupt temporal dependencies or introduce excessive parameters. Our approach combines spectral constraints on the recurrent matrices with element-wise interaction regularization, drawing from prior work on capacity control and recurrent structure regularization. We further integrate temporal activation regularization (TAR) and activation regularization (AR) to penalize large activations and output differences across time steps, while employing weight tying between embedding and softmax layers to reduce parameter count. Theoretical analysis supports the stability and generalization benefits of these constraints. Empirical results across multiple sequence modeling tasks demonstrate improved performance and robustness compared to baseline methods, with consistent gains in perplexity and convergence speed. This framework offers a principled, parameter-efficient alternative to activation-based regularization, with broad applicability to sequence modeling and long-term dependency learning.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Non-monotonically Triggered ASGD (NT-ASGD), a novel adaptive stochastic gradient descent algorithm designed to address the inefficiency of standard ASGD in non-convex optimization by dynamically adjusting validation frequency based on non-monotonic performance. NT-ASGD leverages a non-monotonic interval to trigger validation only when performance deteriorates, thereby reducing unnecessary computation while maintaining convergence guarantees. The algorithm computes a stochastic gradient at each step and, upon reaching a validation interval, evaluates validation perplexity; if the metric exceeds its historical minimum, it records the current step as a trigger point. The method returns a weighted average of weights from the last non-monotonic interval, ensuring robustness to noisy validation metrics. Empirical results demonstrate that NT-ASGD achieves comparable or superior convergence speed and final perplexity on language modeling tasks compared to standard ASGD and other adaptive methods, while significantly reducing validation overhead. This approach offers a practical, theoretically grounded strategy for efficient training of deep recurrent models under non-convex loss landscapes.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), such as long short-term memory networks (LSTMs), serve as a fundamental building block for many sequence learning tasks, including machine translation, language modeling, and question answering. In this paper, we consider the specific problem of word-level language modeling and investigate strategies for regularizing and optimizing LSTM-based models. We propose the weight-dropped LSTM which uses DropConnect on hidden-to-hidden weights as a form of recurrent regularization. Further, we introduce NT-ASGD, a variant of the averaged stochastic gradient method, wherein the averaging trigger is determined using a non-monotonic condition as opposed to being tuned by the user. Using these and other regularization strategies, we achieve state-of-the-art word level perplexities on two data sets: 57.3 on Penn Treebank and 65.8 on WikiText-2. In exploring the effectiveness of a neural cache in conjunction with our proposed model, we achieve an even lower state-of-the-art perplexity of 52.8 on Penn Treebank and 52.0 on WikiText-2.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel Joint Spatial and Temporal Attention Pooling Network (ASTPN) for video-based person re-identification, addressing the challenge of modeling inter-frame dependencies and spatial relevance in dynamic sequences to enhance discriminative representation learning. ASTPN integrates spatial attention to selectively extract informative regions per frame and temporal attention to weight informative frames across the sequence, with both mechanisms jointly guided by distance-based matching signals to enable mutual influence during representation computation. The network is trained end-to-end using triplet loss to optimize feature embeddings under the attention-guided pooling framework. Evaluated on iLIDS-VID, PRID-2011, and MARS, ASTPN achieves state-of-the-art performance across all datasets, consistently outperforming established baselines by significant margins, including in cross-dataset evaluations. This work advances video re-id by demonstrating that attention-aware spatial-temporal pooling improves robustness and accuracy in real-world surveillance scenarios, offering a scalable framework for multi-view and multi-temporal person matching.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose the Jointly Attentive Spatial-Temporal Pooling Network (ASTPN), a novel framework for video-based person re-identification that explicitly models inter-frame dependencies to enhance representation learning. Addressing the challenge of capturing both spatial and temporal cues while leveraging mutual influence between matching instances, ASTPN integrates a spatial attention module to select discriminative regions per frame and a temporal attention module to weight informative frames across the sequence. Both pooling operations are dynamically guided by distance information computed between candidate pairs, enabling the feature extractor to adaptively focus on relevant visual evidence. Evaluated on three benchmark datasets—iLIDS-VID, PRID-2011, and MARS—ASTPN consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving significant gains across all metrics. Cross-dataset experiments further validate its robustness and generalization capability. This work advances the field by demonstrating that joint spatial-temporal attention, informed by matching context, is a critical component for improving video re-id performance.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Person Re-Identification has been viewed as one of the key subproblems of the generic object recognition task. It is also important due to its applications in surveillance, and human-computer interaction communities. Given a query image, the task is to identify a set of matching person images from a pool, usually captured from the same/different cameras, from different viewpoints, at the same/different time points. It is a very challenging task due to the large variations of lighting conditions, viewing angles, body poses and occlusions.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose the Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) block, a novel architectural unit designed to enhance the representational power of convolutional neural networks by explicitly modeling interdependencies between feature channels through dynamic recalibration. Addressing the gap in prior architectures that inadequately capture channel-wise dependencies, especially in deep layers where feature diversity within classes is high, the SE block employs a learnable mechanism that aggregates global spatial context to selectively emphasize informative channels while suppressing redundant ones. This is achieved via a two-layer feed-forward network that computes channel-wise excitation weights, enabling instance-specific recalibration without altering the original convolutional structure. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and tasks demonstrate that SENets incorporating SE blocks achieve state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming baseline models. Theoretically, the SE block provides a principled way to model channel interactions, offering insights into feature importance that can inform model compression and pruning. This work not only advances CNN design but also provides a generalizable framework for improving discriminative feature learning in vision tasks.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose the Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) block, an architectural unit designed to enhance the representational power of convolutional neural networks by enabling dynamic, channel-wise feature recalibration. Addressing the gap in prior architectures that inadequately model inter-channel dependencies, the SE block explicitly learns to recalibrate feature responses using global information, selectively emphasising informative channels while suppressing less useful ones. This is achieved through a two-step mechanism: a squeeze operation that computes global descriptors via global average pooling, followed by an excitation operation that learns channel-wise weights via a fully connected layer with sigmoid activation. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and tasks demonstrate that integrating SE blocks into architectures like ResNet and ResNeXt yields state-of-the-art performance, validating the efficacy of channel recalibration. The learned feature importance values also offer utility for downstream tasks such as network pruning. This work provides a theoretically grounded and empirically successful approach to improving discriminative feature learning, with broad implications for model compression and other vision tasks requiring strong feature selection.
detailed_results.reference_summary: The central building block of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) is the convolution operator, which enables networks to construct informative features by fusing both spatial and channel-wise information within local receptive fields at each layer. A broad range of prior research has investigated the spatial component of this relationship, seeking to strengthen the representational power of a CNN by enhancing the quality of spatial encodings throughout its feature hierarchy. In this work, we focus instead on the channel relationship and propose a novel architectural unit, which we term the “Squeeze-and-Excitation” (SE) block, that adaptively recalibrates channel-wise feature responses by explicitly modelling interdependencies between channels. We show that these blocks can be stacked together to form SENet architectures that generalise extremely effectively across different datasets. We further demonstrate that SE blocks bring significant improvements in performance for existing state-of-the-art CNNs at slight additional computational cost. Squeeze-and-Excitation Networks formed the foundation of our ILSVRC 2017 classification submission which won first place and reduced the top-5 error to 2.251%, surpassing the winning entry of 2016 by a relative improvement of ∼25%. Models and code are available at https://github.com/hujie-frank/SENet.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel framework for learning node representations in arbitrary graph structures, addressing the limitations of spectral methods that suffer from high computational cost and non-localized filters, while also extending beyond the inductive capacity of earlier recursive neural networks. Our method, inspired by GraphSAGE, employs a neighborhood sampling strategy where each node aggregates features from a fixed-size set of neighbors using a learnable aggregator—such as mean pooling or recurrent neural network transformations—enabling scalable, inductive representation learning. Unlike spectral approaches, our framework operates in the spatial domain, preserving locality and reducing computational overhead. Evaluated across multiple large-scale inductive benchmarks, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance in node classification tasks, demonstrating robust generalization and efficiency. Theoretically, we show that the aggregator design allows for expressive power sufficient to capture complex relational patterns. This work advances the field by providing a practical, scalable, and theoretically grounded approach to graph representation learning, with broad applicability in social networks, recommendation systems, and molecular modeling.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a novel framework for learning node representations on arbitrary graphs, addressing the challenge of extending convolutional neural network principles to non-grid structured data. Our method, inspired by GraphSAGE, computes node embeddings inductively by sampling fixed-size neighborhoods and applying a learnable aggregator—such as mean or concatenation—over neighbor features. Crucially, we introduce a mechanism for aggregating neighbor representations using a parameterized function that combines both feature and structural information, enabling spatially localized and scalable message passing. Evaluated on standard benchmarks including Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, and PPI, our model demonstrates competitive performance in both transductive and inductive settings, particularly excelling on large-scale, multi-label PPI data. Theoretical analysis confirms the locality and inductive bias of our approach, which generalizes well across graph structures. This work advances the state-of-the-art in graph representation learning by unifying neighborhood aggregation with structured message passing, offering a scalable and theoretically grounded solution for complex graph-based tasks.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present graph attention networks (GATs), novel neural network architectures that operate on graph-structured data, leveraging masked self-attentional layers to address the shortcomings of prior methods based on graph convolutions or their approximations. By stacking layers in which nodes are able to attend over their neighborhoods’ features, we enable (implicitly) specifying different weights to different nodes in a neighborhood, without requiring any kind of costly matrix operation (such as inversion) or depending on knowing the graph structure upfront. In this way, we address several key challenges of spectral-based graph neural networks simultaneously, and make our model readily applicable to inductive as well as transductive problems. Our GAT models have achieved or matched state-of-the-art results across four established transductive and inductive graph benchmarks: the Cora, Citeseer and Pubmed citation network datasets, as well as a protein-protein interaction dataset (wherein test graphs remain unseen during training).

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel training framework for variational autoencoders with discrete latent variables, addressing the persistent “posterior collapse” issue that plagues VAEs with powerful decoders, where latent variables are effectively ignored. Building on the success of VQ-VAE in modeling high-dimensional, semantically meaningful features—such as objects in images or phonemes in speech—we introduce a multi-sample objective optimized via VIMCO to accelerate convergence and stabilize training. Our method leverages discrete representations, which are inherently more suitable for modalities like language, speech, and image-text alignment, and are essential for complex reasoning and predictive modeling. Experimental results demonstrate improved latent utilization and reconstruction quality compared to standard VAEs and VQ-VAE baselines, validated across diverse discrete data domains. Theoretically, our approach ensures that discrete latents capture meaningful structure rather than noise, enabling more interpretable and generalizable representations. This work advances the feasibility of discrete latent modeling in deep learning, offering a scalable solution for tasks requiring symbolic reasoning and structured data representation.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a novel training framework for variational autoencoders with discrete latent variables, addressing the persistent challenge of posterior collapse that plagues VAEs with powerful decoders. Our method leverages discrete representations—natural for discrete modalities like language, speech, and symbolic image descriptions—to model high-level, semantically meaningful features while avoiding overfitting to noise or local details. We build upon VIMCO’s multi-sample objective to improve convergence and introduce a structured training procedure that ensures latent variables are actively utilized. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach effectively mitigates posterior collapse, enabling the model to learn compact, meaningful discrete codes that capture essential data structure. Theoretical analysis confirms that the method promotes better latent variable utilization compared to standard VAE training. This work advances the feasibility of discrete latent spaces in deep generative models, offering a more principled approach for tasks requiring complex reasoning, planning, and predictive modeling.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Learning useful representations without supervision remains a key challenge in machine learning. In this paper, we propose a simple yet powerful generative model that learns such discrete representations. Our model, the Vector Quantised-Variational AutoEncoder (VQ-VAE), differs from VAEs in two key ways: the encoder network outputs discrete, rather than continuous, codes; and the prior is learnt rather than static. In order to learn a discrete latent representation, we incorporate ideas from vector quantisation (VQ). Using the VQ method allows the model to circumvent issues of “posterior collapse” — where the latents are ignored when they are paired with a powerful autoregressive decoder — typically observed in the VAE framework. Pairing these representations with an autoregressive prior, the model can generate high quality images, videos, and speech as well as doing high quality speaker conversion and unsupervised learning of phonemes, providing further evidence of the utility of the learnt representations.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel convolutional module designed for efficient mobile inference, leveraging standard operations across modern neural frameworks while significantly reducing memory footprint by avoiding full materialization of intermediate tensors—critical for embedded systems with limited fast cache. This architecture enables high-performance models that outperform state-of-the-art on ImageNet, COCO, and VOC benchmarks, with particular emphasis on memory-efficient deployment. A key theoretical insight is that linear bottlenecks, though strictly less expressive than non-linear models, empirically enhance performance by preserving information in low-dimensional spaces, suggesting non-linearities may degrade representational capacity under constraints. Our method decouples input/output domains from transformation expressiveness, facilitating modular analysis and optimization. Trained and evaluated across standard computer vision tasks, the approach demonstrates consistent gains without architectural complexity, offering a scalable foundation for efficient, high-accuracy mobile vision systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a novel convolutional module architecture designed to enable highly efficient, memory-optimized inference on mobile and embedded hardware, addressing the critical need for lightweight models without sacrificing performance. The core design features a sequence of 1x1 convolutions, ReLU6 activations, and a 3x3 depthwise separable convolution, followed by a linear bottleneck that decouples input/output domains from transformation expressiveness. This structure leverages standard operations across frameworks while minimizing intermediate tensor materialization, thereby reducing main memory access and enhancing cache efficiency. Experimental results on ImageNet, COCO, and VOC benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance gains, particularly highlighting that linear bottlenecks—though theoretically less expressive than non-linear variants—empirically improve accuracy by preserving information in low-dimensional spaces, suggesting non-linearities may introduce detrimental information loss. This framework provides a theoretically tractable and practically deployable foundation for efficient mobile vision models.
detailed_results.reference_summary: In this paper we describe a new mobile architecture, MobileNetV2, that improves the state of the art performance of mobile models on multiple tasks and benchmarks as well as across a spectrum of different model sizes. We also describe efficient ways of applying these mobile models to object detection in a novel framework we call SSDLite. Additionally, we demonstrate how to build mobile semantic segmentation models through a reduced form of DeepLabv3 which we call Mobile DeepLabv3.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a set of efficiency-enhancing methods for sampling from sequential generative models in text-to-speech synthesis, addressing the prohibitively large computational cost \( T(\mathbf{u}) \) that scales with input size \( |\mathbf{u}| \), model depth \( N \), per-operation computation \( c(op_i) \), and overhead \( d(op_i) \). Our framework reduces the impact of each factor while preserving output quality, enabling orders-of-magnitude faster sampling without degradation. Key techniques include architectural optimizations to minimize per-layer overhead and computation, as well as sampling strategies that amortize cost across operations. Evaluated on a single-speaker North-American English TTS dataset using predicted linguistic features, our methods demonstrate significant speedup with minimal quality loss, measured by perceptual and waveform fidelity metrics. Theoretical analysis confirms that our approach maintains model capacity while reducing asymptotic complexity. This work advances efficient inference for sequential models, with implications for real-time deployment in speech, vision, and other generative domains requiring high-fidelity, low-latency output.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a suite of architectural optimizations to accelerate sampling in sequential generative models for text-to-speech synthesis, addressing the prohibitive computational cost of T(u) driven by large input size |u|, deep architectures (large N), high per-operation computation c(op_i), and overhead d(op_i). Our methods reduce the impact of each factor while preserving output quality, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups. We benchmark against WaveRNN and evaluate variants including Sparse WR 384, Sparse WR Mobile, and Subscale WR 1024, demonstrating significant improvements in efficiency without quality degradation. Results show Sparse WR Mobile achieves the highest speedup with a 0.40 ± 0.09 NLL reduction over WaveRNN-896, while maintaining competitive performance. Theoretical and empirical analysis confirms that sparsity can be applied effectively even under parameter constraints, as shown by NLL vs. sparsity curves indicating diminishing returns beyond a certain threshold. These techniques enable practical deployment of high-fidelity sequential models in resource-constrained environments, bridging the gap between state-of-the-art quality and real-time inference.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Sequential models achieve state-of-the-art results in audio, visual and textual domains with respect to both estimating the data distribution and generating high-quality samples. Efficient sampling for this class of models has however remained an elusive problem. With a focus on text-to-speech synthesis, we describe a set of general techniques for reducing sampling time while maintaining high output quality. We first describe a single-layer recurrent neural network, the WavRNN, with a dual softmax layer that matches the quality of the state-of-the-art WaveNet model. The compact form of the network makes it possible to generate 24 kHz 16-bit audio 4× faster than real time on a GPU. Second, we apply a weight pruning technique to reduce the number of weights in the WavRNN. We find that, for a constant number of parameters, large sparse networks perform better than small dense networks and this relationship holds for sparsity levels beyond 96%. The small number of weights in a Sparse WavRNN makes it possible to sample high-fidelity audio on a mobile CPU in real time. Finally, we propose a new generation scheme based on subscaling that folds a long sequence into a batch of shorter sequences and allows one to generate multiple samples at once. The Subscale WavRNN produces 16 samples per step without loss of quality and offers an orthogonal method for increasing sampling efficiency.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose to train Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) as policy functions to solve combinatorial optimization problems, particularly routing problems, by learning decision-making heuristics directly from data. Unlike classical exact methods or rule-based heuristics, our approach parameterizes solution-selection policies end-to-end, enabling adaptive, data-driven optimization akin to DNNs’ success in perception and prediction tasks. Leveraging Reinforcement Learning, the DNNs are trained to select optimal or near-optimal routes in large combinatorial spaces, effectively solving problems equivalent to the Travelling Salesman Problem. Experimental results demonstrate significant performance gains over traditional heuristics, with competitive or superior solution quality across multiple routing benchmarks. The framework’s flexibility allows transfer across problem instances and scales efficiently with problem size. This work bridges machine learning and operations research, offering a generalizable paradigm for automating algorithm design in complex optimization domains, where human-engineered heuristics are replaced by learned, adaptive policies.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose to train Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) as policy functions to solve combinatorial optimization problems, specifically routing problems, by learning to select optimal solutions from a vast combinatorial space. Building on the success of DNNs in prediction and Reinforcement Learning in decision-making, we frame routing problems—such as the Travelling Salesman Problem—as decision tasks where policies are learned through interaction with the problem environment. Our framework employs a graph neural network architecture with multi-head attention and feed-forward layers to encode node and graph embeddings, enabling the model to reason over structural dependencies. Policies are trained via reinforcement learning, using both Exponential and Rollout baselines to estimate value functions and guide policy updates. Experimental results on TSP instances of varying sizes demonstrate that our learned policies consistently outperform classical heuristics and baseline RL methods, achieving competitive or superior solution quality with significantly reduced computational time. This approach establishes a generalizable paradigm for learning high-performing, data-driven algorithms for complex combinatorial optimization, bridging the gap between machine learning and algorithmic engineering.
detailed_results.reference_summary: The recently presented idea to learn heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems is promising as it can save costly development. However, to push this idea towards practical implementation, we need better models and better ways of training. We contribute in both directions: we propose a model based on attention layers with benefits over the Pointer Network and we show how to train this model using REINFORCE with a simple baseline based on a deterministic greedy rollout, which we find is more efficient than using a value function. We significantly improve over recent learned heuristics for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), getting close to optimal results for problems up to 100 nodes. With the same hyperparameters, we learn strong heuristics for two variants of the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP), the Orienteering Problem (OP) and (a stochastic variant of) the Prize Collecting TSP (PCTSP), outperforming a wide range of baselines and getting results close to highly optimized and specialized algorithms.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose an improved YOLO-based object detection framework that leverages dimension clusters as anchor boxes, adapting the YOLO9000 architecture to enhance localization accuracy. The system predicts four box coordinates—\( t_x \), \( t_y \), \( t_w \), \( t_h \)—relative to predefined priors, using sum of squared error loss during training. Class predictions are handled via independent logistic classifiers rather than softmax, optimized with binary cross-entropy loss, enabling efficient multilabel classification without performance degradation. To refine detection, we adopt a single bounding box prior per ground truth object, avoiding redundant predictions and reducing loss for unassigned anchors. Evaluation on COCO reveals robust performance using nine anchor clusters: (10×13), (16×30), ..., (373×326), with a 0.5 IoU threshold for filtering predictions. The model’s design is replicated across scales to maintain consistency. Our approach demonstrates that small architectural refinements and targeted loss functions yield measurable gains without introducing complex mechanisms. This work contributes a streamlined, effective detection pipeline suitable for real-time applications and provides a practical baseline for future anchor-based object detectors.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose an improved YOLO architecture that enhances detection accuracy and efficiency by leveraging dimension clusters as anchor boxes, predicting bounding box coordinates via \( t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h \) relative to cell offsets and prior dimensions, and employing multilabel logistic classifiers for class prediction without softmax. Training utilizes sum of squared error for localization and binary cross-entropy for class probabilities, with a threshold of 0.5 for objectness assignment and loss computation. The system is designed to assign a single prior per ground truth object, avoiding unnecessary loss for unassigned predictions. Experimental results on COCO demonstrate competitive performance, achieving 32.8 mAP with 22 ms inference time, outperforming prior methods like RetinaNet-50 and SSD321 while maintaining low latency. The architecture’s efficiency is further validated by its scalable design, featuring residual blocks and multi-scale prediction, enabling high accuracy with minimal computational overhead. This work advances real-time object detection by optimizing anchor selection, loss functions, and prediction strategy, offering a practical balance between speed and precision for deployment in resource-constrained environments.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present some updates to YOLO! We made a bunch of little design changes to make it better. We also trained this new network that’s pretty swell. It’s a little bigger than last time but more accurate. It’s still fast though, don’t worry. At 320 × 320 YOLOv3 runs in 22 ms at 28.2 mAP, as accurate as SSD but three times faster. When we look at the old .5 IOU mAP detection metric YOLOv3 is quite good. It achieves 57.9 AP50 in 51 ms on a Titan X, compared to 57.5 AP50 in 198 ms by RetinaNet, similar performance but 3.8× faster. As always, all the code is online at https://pjreddie.com/yolo/.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose an improved YOLO-based object detection framework that leverages dimension clusters as anchor boxes, adapting the YOLO9000 architecture to enhance localization accuracy through per-cell coordinate predictions (tx, ty, tw, th) relative to image offsets and prior box dimensions. The system employs nine predefined anchor clusters on COCO, predicts multi-class labels via independent logistic classifiers rather than softmax, and uses binary cross-entropy for classification and sum-of-squared-error for regression during training. To refine detection, predictions overlapping ground truth objects by at least 0.5 IoU are retained, while unassigned anchors incur no loss, promoting efficient training.
