The model is extended across multiple scales, maintaining consistent architecture. Experimental results demonstrate improved performance through small architectural refinements and efficient loss design, without requiring complex softmax or multi-scale feature fusion. This approach offers a streamlined, effective detection pipeline suitable for real-time applications, emphasizing practical gains over theoretical novelty.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper introduces an improved YOLO-based object detection framework that leverages dimension clusters as anchor boxes, building upon YOLO9000. The system predicts bounding box coordinates via four regression outputs—\( t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h \)—transformed using sigmoid and exponential functions relative to predefined prior dimensions and grid cell offsets. Class predictions employ independent logistic classifiers with binary cross-entropy loss, avoiding softmax. During training, coordinate predictions are optimized via sum of squared error, while objectness and class losses are only applied when a bounding box prior is assigned to a ground truth object, using a 0.5 IoU threshold for assignment. The framework is extended to multiple scales, with each scale applying the same design. Evaluated on COCO, the method achieves competitive performance: 32.8 mAP at 22 ms inference time, outperforming YOLOv3-320 and RetinaNet-50 while maintaining efficiency. This approach demonstrates that refined anchor clustering and selective loss assignment can yield high accuracy with minimal computational overhead, offering a practical balance for real-time applications.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present some updates to YOLO! We made a bunch of little design changes to make it better. We also trained this new network that’s pretty swell. It’s a little bigger than last time but more accurate. It’s still fast though, don’t worry. At 320 × 320 YOLOv3 runs in 22 ms at 28.2 mAP, as accurate as SSD but three times faster. When we look at the old .5 IOU mAP detection metric YOLOv3 is quite good. It achieves 57.9 AP50 in 51 ms on a Titan X, compared to 57.5 AP50 in 198 ms by RetinaNet, similar performance but 3.8× faster. As always, all the code is online at https://pjreddie.com/yolo/.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose Glow, a novel generative flow model that addresses the dual challenges of data efficiency and generalization in machine learning by leveraging invertible 1×1 convolutions within a multi-scale architecture. Our framework introduces three core components—ActNorm, a data-dependent scale and bias layer; a nonlinear transformation via a shallow convolutional neural network; and an invertible coupling layer—each with analytically tractable log-determinants for efficient likelihood computation. Training proceeds via maximum likelihood estimation, enabling direct sampling and density evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in generating high-fidelity synthetic images, including realistic celebrity portraits, while maintaining computational tractability. Theoretical guarantees on invertibility and density estimation ensure robustness to distributional shifts. This work advances generative modeling by combining expressive capacity with efficient inference, offering a scalable foundation for tasks requiring few-shot learning and contextual generalization.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Glow, a novel generative flow model designed to address the critical challenges of data efficiency and generalization in machine learning. Building upon invertible neural networks, Glow introduces a multi-scale architecture that combines three core components—Actnorm, invertible 1×1 convolutions, and affine coupling layers—each with analytically tractable inverses and log-determinants. These components are stacked and recursively applied across spatial dimensions via a squeeze operation, enabling efficient, high-fidelity sampling and likelihood computation. The model is trained end-to-end using maximum likelihood estimation, with Actnorm providing data-dependent initialization to stabilize learning. Experimental results demonstrate that Glow achieves state-of-the-art performance on image generation tasks, producing high-fidelity samples while maintaining computational tractability. This framework offers a theoretically grounded approach to generative modeling, providing a scalable and interpretable architecture for complex data distributions, with implications for improving data efficiency and robustness in downstream applications.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Flow-based generative models (Dinh et al., 2014) are conceptually attractive due to tractability of the exact log-likelihood, tractability of exact latent-variable inference, and parallelizability of both training and synthesis. In this paper we propose Glow, a simple type of generative flow using an invertible \(1 \times 1\) convolution. Using our method we demonstrate a significant improvement in log-likelihood on standard benchmarks. Perhaps most strikingly, we demonstrate that a generative model optimized towards the plain log-likelihood objective is capable of efficient realistic-looking synthesis and manipulation of large images. The code for our model is available at https://github.com/openai/glow.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose ShuffleNet V2, a refined CNN architecture designed to achieve superior speed-accuracy trade-offs under constrained computational budgets, addressing the gap between theoretical FLOPs and practical latency. Building on group and depth-wise convolutions, ShuffleNet V2 introduces channel shuffle operations and inverted residual blocks to enhance feature reuse while reducing redundancy. Its training leverages a novel channel splitting strategy and a lightweight attention mechanism to improve representational capacity without increasing computational cost. Evaluated on ImageNet and benchmark datasets, ShuffleNet V2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency performance, with up to 4x faster inference than prior lightweight models while maintaining competitive top-1 accuracy. Theoretical analysis confirms its computational efficiency, and empirical results validate its robustness across diverse hardware platforms. This work provides practical guidelines for efficient CNN design, enabling deployment in latency-sensitive applications such as autonomous driving and mobile vision systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper introduces ShuffleNet V2, a CNN architecture designed to achieve superior speed-accuracy trade-offs under constrained computational budgets, addressing the critical gap between FLOPs-based complexity metrics and real-world latency. We propose a refined channel shuffle mechanism and a more efficient inverted residual block, alongside a novel training strategy that leverages channel-wise attention to enhance feature representation. Our method systematically improves upon prior lightweight designs, including MobileNet V2 and ShuffleNet V1, by optimizing both computational efficiency and accuracy. Extensive experiments across GPU and ARM platforms demonstrate that ShuffleNet V2 achieves state-of-the-art performance: for instance, on ARM, it delivers 57.6 images/sec at 15.1% top-1 accuracy with only 9.108 MFLOPs, outperforming competitors across all efficiency tiers. These results validate our approach’s practicality for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices, establishing a new standard for efficient CNN design.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Currently, the neural network architecture design is mostly guided by the indirect metric of computation complexity, i.e., FLOPs. However, the direct metric, e.g., speed, also depends on the other factors such as memory access cost and platform characteristics. Thus, this work proposes to evaluate the direct metric on the target platform, beyond only considering FLOPs. Based on a series of controlled experiments, this work derives several practical guidelines for efficient network design. Accordingly, a new architecture is presented, called ShuffleNet V2. Comprehensive ablation experiments verify that our model is the state-of-the-art in terms of speed and accuracy tradeoff."

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel end-to-end deep learning architecture for graph classification that addresses the challenge of effectively representing complex graph structures through recursive neighborhood aggregation, as foundational to Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Our method integrates message passing with attention mechanisms to dynamically weight neighbor contributions, enhancing structural representation, while incorporating dropout for regularization to mitigate overfitting. The architecture is trained via backpropagation across multiple layers, culminating in graph-level pooling through node feature summation. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate superior performance over state-of-the-art GNNs, including Graph Attention Networks and Jumping Knowledge Networks, with statistically significant improvements in accuracy and robustness. Theoretical analysis confirms that our approach preserves local structural information while enabling global graph representation. This work advances the field by providing a scalable, theoretically grounded framework for graph classification, with implications for applications in chemistry, biology, and social network analysis where graph-structured data is prevalent.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper introduces a novel framework for graph representation learning that addresses the challenge of effectively capturing structural information across diverse graph types, such as molecules and networks. It proposes a multi-layer architecture where each node aggregates features from its neighbors via a recursive message-passing scheme, followed by a pooling operation to generate a graph-level representation. The core innovation lies in the introduction of a multi-layer aggregation strategy that combines the outputs of multiple layers using distinct pooling functions—specifically, sum, mean, and max—before final classification. This approach, termed SUM–MLP, is evaluated across ten benchmark datasets, demonstrating superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines including GIN, GCN, and GraphSAGE. The results show that the SUM–MLP variant achieves the highest accuracy on all datasets, with a particularly significant gain on NC11, where it attains 82.7% accuracy. The method's theoretical grounding in aggregation functions and its empirical robustness underscore its potential for scalable, high-accuracy graph classification tasks.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are an effective framework for representation learning of graphs. GNNs follow a neighborhood aggregation scheme, where the representation vector of a node is computed by recursively aggregating and transforming representation vectors of its neighboring nodes. Many GNN variants have been proposed and have achieved state-of-the-art results on both node and graph classification tasks. However, despite GNNs revolutionizing graph representation learning, there is limited understanding of their representational properties and limitations. Here, we present a theoretical framework for analyzing the expressive power of GNNs to capture different graph structures. Our results characterize the discriminative power of popular GNN variants, such as Graph Convolutional Networks and GraphSAGE, and show that they cannot learn to distinguish certain simple graph structures. We then develop a simple architecture that is provably the most expressive among the class of GNNs and is as powerful as the Weisfeiler-Lehman graph isomorphism test. We empirically validate our theoretical findings on a number of graph classification benchmarks, and demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance."

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose BERT, a deep bidirectional transformer model that leverages masked language modeling for unsupervised pre-training to capture contextualized representations, addressing the gap between unidirectional pre-training and the need for bidirectional understanding in NLP tasks. Unlike prior approaches such as ELMo or unidirectional autoregressive models, BERT’s architecture enables simultaneous left- and right-context encoding through a transformer encoder with masked inputs, allowing richer contextual representation without requiring task-specific architectures. Evaluated across eleven NLP tasks—including sentence-level inference and token-level named entity recognition—BERT achieves state-of-the-art performance, often surpassing task-specific models, and demonstrates robust generalization via a simple fine-tuning procedure. The model’s effectiveness is further validated by its open-source availability and consistent gains across diverse benchmarks. This work establishes fine-tuning as a powerful paradigm for transferring pre-trained representations, reducing reliance on hand-crafted features and significantly advancing the efficiency and performance of downstream NLP systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose BERT, a novel pre-training framework that leverages bidirectional context through masked language modeling to generate deep, contextualized representations, addressing the limitation of unidirectional models like those in Radford et al. (2018). BERT's architecture employs a transformer encoder with two key objectives: Masked Language Model (MLM) for pre-training on unlabeled text, and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) to learn sentence relationships. These representations are then fine-tuned with minimal task-specific modifications for downstream tasks. Experimental results across eleven NLP benchmarks—including MNLI, QQP, and SST-2—demonstrate BERT's state-of-the-art performance, with BERT_LARGE achieving an average score of 82.1, outperforming prior SOTA models like BiLSTM+ELMo+Attn (75.1) and OpenAI GPT (79.6). This approach significantly reduces the need for task-specific architectures, establishing a new paradigm for transfer learning in NLP by unifying pre-training and fine-tuning under a single, generalizable framework.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models (Peters et al., 2018a; Radford et al., 2018), BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks, such as question answering and language inference, without substantial task-specific architecture modifications.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a generator architecture that embeds input latent codes into an intermediate latent space to enable disentangled representation of factors of variation, addressing the inherent entanglement in traditional GAN latent spaces constrained by training data density. Our method introduces two novel automated metrics—perceptual path length and linear separability—to quantify disentanglement, alongside a third metric measuring binary attribute separability via linear hyperplanes. Experimental results demonstrate that our generator achieves more linear, less entangled representations compared to traditional and style-based architectures, with improved perceptual path length and FID scores. Notably, while mapping networks enhance perceptual path length across architectures, they exacerbate entanglement in the input latent space (measured by \( l_{\mathcal{Z}} \)), confirming our theoretical claim. These findings establish a principled framework for evaluating and improving latent space disentanglement in generative models, with implications for controllability, interpretability, and downstream applications requiring factor-specific manipulation.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a novel generator architecture that leverages an intermediate latent space to achieve more disentangled representations of image factors of variation, addressing the inherent entanglement in traditional input latent spaces constrained by training data density. Our method introduces two automated metrics—perceptual path length and linear separability—to quantify disentanglement, enabling direct comparison with conventional architectures. Experimental results demonstrate that our generator produces representations that are more linear and less entangled, as measured by improved path lengths and enhanced separability via linear hyperplanes. We further show that both traditional and style-based generators benefit from mapping networks, with deeper networks improving perceptual quality and FID scores, while revealing that the input latent space remains arbitrarily entangled, as evidenced by degraded linear separability metrics. This work provides a theoretically grounded and empirically validated framework for evaluating and enhancing latent space disentanglement in generative models, with implications for controllability and interpretability in image synthesis.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We propose an alternative generator architecture for generative adversarial networks, borrowing from style transfer literature. The new architecture leads to an automatically learned, unsupervised separation of high-level attributes (e.g., pose and identity when trained on human faces) and stochastic variation in the generated images (e.g., freckles, hair), and it enables intuitive, scale-specific control of the synthesis. The new generator improves the state-of-the-art in terms of traditional distribution quality metrics, leads to demonstrably better interpolation properties, and also better disentangles the latent factors of variation. To quantify interpolation quality and disentanglement, we propose two new, automated methods that are applicable to any generator architecture. Finally, we introduce a new, highly varied and high-quality dataset of human faces.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: This paper addresses the challenge of designing scalable and theoretically grounded graph neural networks (GNNs) that effectively model spectral graph convolutions with trainable non-linear filters, building upon foundational work by Bruna et al. (2013) and Henaff et al. (2015). We propose a framework that explicitly leverages spectral graph filtering in the frequency domain, enabling precise control over filter design and transferability across graph structures, as analyzed by Levie et al. (2019a). Our method integrates variational auto-encoding principles (Kipf & Welling, 2016b) with spectral convolutional layers to learn low-pass filters that preserve structural information while enabling expressive node representations. Experimental results on semi-supervised node classification tasks demonstrate superior performance over baseline GNNs, with consistent gains across diverse graph benchmarks. Theoretical analysis confirms that our filters operate as low-pass filters in the spectral domain, aligning with Maehara (2019), while maintaining non-linear expressivity. This work advances the understanding of GNNs as spectral operators and provides a principled approach for transferable, scalable graph learning.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper addresses the challenge of designing graph neural networks (GNNs) that effectively capture complex, multi-scale spectral interactions in graph-structured data while maintaining strong generalization. We propose ARMA Graph Convolution, a novel framework that explicitly models graph signals as linear combinations of autoregressive (AR) and moving average (MA) components, enabling the network to learn non-linear, trainable spectral filters that operate across multiple scales. The architecture employs a stack of skip-connected convolutional layers, each applying a trainable filter to the graph signal in the spectral domain, followed by a non-linear activation. We demonstrate that this approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on node classification benchmarks, consistently outperforming established methods including GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN, and ChebNet. Specifically, ARMA achieves 90.5% accuracy on PPI, surpassing all competitors. Theoretically, we show that ARMA filters are inherently low-pass, which facilitates stable and interpretable learning. This work establishes a principled spectral framework for GNNs, offering a new perspective on the design of scalable and transferable graph convolutional operators.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Popular graph neural networks implement convolution operations on graphs based on polynomial spectral filters. In this paper, we propose a novel graph convolutional layer inspired by the auto-regressive moving average (ARMA) filter that, compared to polynomial ones, provides a more flexible frequency response, is more robust to noise, and better captures the global graph structure. We propose a graph neural network implementation of the ARMA filter with a recursive and distributed formulation, obtaining a convolutional layer that is efficient to train, localized in the node space, and can be transferred to new graphs at test time. We perform a spectral analysis to study the filtering effect of the proposed ARMA layer and report experiments on four downstream tasks: semi-supervised node classification, graph signal classification, graph classification, and graph regression. Results show that the proposed ARMA layer brings significant improvements over graph neural networks based on polynomial filters.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a framework for generating structured data by extending variational autoencoders to explicitly model latent space geometry and conditional dependencies, enabling probabilistic reasoning over low-dimensional manifolds. Building on variational inference principles, our method introduces a generalized denoising autoencoder architecture that jointly optimizes latent representation quality and generation fidelity through adversarial regularization and structured latent sampling. Experimental results across computer vision, NLP, and molecular generation tasks demonstrate superior sample validity and scoring metrics compared to baseline VAEs and CVAEs, with the top-performing models achieving over 90% valid samples and mean scores exceeding 0.85 on standardized benchmarks. Theoretical analysis confirms convergence properties under Markov chain Monte Carlo diagnostics, while empirical studies validate latent space optimization via gradient-based sampling. This work advances generative modeling by unifying representation learning, conditional generation, and structural validity, offering scalable tools for applications requiring interpretable, high-fidelity synthesis.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper addresses the challenge of generating high-fidelity, diverse, and structurally valid samples from complex data distributions, a core limitation of standard variational autoencoders (VAEs). We propose a framework that integrates structured generation and latent space optimization, leveraging techniques such as generalized denoising autoencoders and latent space regularization to enhance sample quality and diversity. Our method systematically evaluates performance across multiple benchmarks—MNIST, CIFAR, and CelebA—using metrics including reconstruction error, sample diversity (measured by GMM), and interpolation quality. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms baseline models, including VAE, CV-VAE, and RAE variants, particularly in generating valid, high-scoring samples and achieving superior interpolation fidelity. The findings validate the efficacy of our structured generation strategy and latent space optimization, offering a robust framework for applications requiring precise control over generated outputs, such as molecular design and computer vision.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) provide a theoretically-backed and popular framework for deep generative models. However, learning a VAE from data poses still unanswered theoretical questions and considerable practical challenges. In this work, we propose an alternative framework for generative modeling that is simpler, easier to train, and deterministic, yet has many of the advantages of VAEs. We observe that sampling a stochastic encoder in a Gaussian VAE can be interpreted as simply injecting noise into the input of a deterministic decoder. We investigate how substituting this kind of stochasticity, with other explicit and implicit regularization schemes, can lead to an equally smooth and meaningful latent space without forcing it to conform to an arbitrarily chosen prior. To retrieve a generative mechanism to sample new data, we introduce an ex-post density estimation step that can be readily applied also to existing VAEs, improving their sample quality. We show, in a rigorous empirical study, that the proposed regularized deterministic autoencoders are able to generate samples that are comparable to, or better than, those of VAEs and more powerful alternatives when applied to images as well as to structured data such as molecules.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose MobileNetV3 Large and Small, next-generation mobile neural networks designed to deliver high accuracy with minimal computational and power overhead, enabling efficient on-device computer vision. Addressing the challenge of balancing model performance with hardware constraints, we integrate automated architecture search with novel design principles including inverted residual blocks, adaptive activation functions, and a refined bottleneck structure. Trained on ImageNet with a focus on latency and energy efficiency, the models demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across diverse hardware platforms, as measured by MAAdds, top-1 accuracy, and Pixel 1 latency. Experimental results show that MobileNetV3 variants with multipliers 0.75, 1, and 1.25 achieve optimal performance frontiers for 224-pixel input resolution, with TFLite inference on a single large core. These models significantly outperform prior mobile architectures while preserving battery life, establishing a new benchmark for efficient neural networks in resource-constrained environments and providing a scalable framework for future mobile AI deployment.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper introduces MobileNetV3 Large and Small, next-generation mobile neural network architectures designed to deliver high accuracy with minimal computational cost for on-device computer vision. Addressing the challenge of balancing model efficiency with performance on resource-constrained devices, the authors propose a framework that integrates automated architecture search with novel design principles, including a reimagined inverted residual block and a new nonlinear activation function. The models are trained using a combination of curriculum learning and label smoothing to enhance generalization and robustness. Experimental results demonstrate that MobileNetV3 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, as evidenced by its superior performance on the ImageNet dataset measured in terms of top-1 accuracy and MAAdds, and by its low latency on Pixel 1 hardware using TFLite inference. The proposed models, available in multipliers of 0.35, 0.5, 0.75, 1, and 1.25 for input resolution 224, offer a scalable solution for diverse mobile platforms, significantly advancing the field of efficient neural networks for privacy-preserving, battery-conscious on-device applications.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present the next generation of MobileNets based on a combination of complementary search techniques as well as a novel architecture design. MobileNetV3 is tuned to mobile phone CPUs through a combination of hardware-aware network architecture search (NAS) complemented by the NetAdapt algorithm and then subsequently improved through novel architecture advances. This paper starts the exploration of how automated search algorithms and network design can work together to harness complementary approaches improving the overall state of the art. Through this process we create two new MobileNet models for release: MobileNetV3-Large and MobileNetV3-Small which are targeted for high and low resource use cases. These models are then adapted and applied to the tasks of object detection and semantic segmentation. For the task of semantic segmentation (or any dense pixel prediction), we propose a new efficient segmentation decoder Lite Reduced Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (LR-ASPP). We achieve new state of the art results for mobile classification, detection and segmentation. MobileNetV3-Large is 3.2% more accurate on ImageNet classification while reducing latency by 20% compared to MobileNetV2. MobileNetV3-Small is 6.6% more accurate compared to a MobileNetV2 model with comparable latency. MobileNetV3-Large detection is over 25% faster at roughly the same accuracy as MobileNetV2 on COCO detection. MobileNetV3-Large LR-ASPP is 34% faster than MobileNetV2 R-ASPP at similar accuracy for Cityscapes segmentation.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose VQ-VAE-2, a two-level variational autoencoder architecture designed to generate diverse, high-fidelity images by decoupling discrete latent representations from continuous latent spaces. Addressing the challenge of balancing perceptual quality with semantic diversity in generative models, VQ-VAE-2 employs a vector quantization module to enforce discrete latent codes while using a hierarchical encoder-decoder structure to capture multi-scale image features. Trained end-to-end with a reconstruction loss and commitment loss, the model learns to preserve fine-grained details while enabling flexible, class-conditional generation. Experimental results on ImageNet-256 and FFHQ-1024 demonstrate superior sample quality and diversity compared to prior methods, as measured by FID and classification accuracy scores. Theoretical analysis confirms that the discrete latent space promotes disentangled representations, enhancing controllability. VQ-VAE-2’s architecture and training procedure offer a scalable framework for conditional image generation, with implications for applications ranging from domain editing to artistic manipulation, bridging the gap between fidelity and diversity in deep generative modeling.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose VQ-VAE-2, a two-stage hierarchical generative model designed to produce diverse, high-fidelity images by decoupling representation learning from generative modeling. The first stage trains a VQ-VAE to learn discrete, class-conditional latent representations, while the second stage trains a PixelCNN prior on these quantized codes to enable high-resolution, sample-efficient generation. This architecture explicitly separates the encoder’s role in learning meaningful, disentangled representations from the decoder’s role in reconstructing detailed imagery, addressing limitations in prior models that struggle with both fidelity and diversity simultaneously. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-256 and FFHQ-1024 demonstrate that VQ-VAE-2 generates visually superior samples compared to state-of-the-art models, achieving high classification accuracy scores on generated images and producing diverse, class-conditional outputs. The method’s modular design and training procedure offer a theoretically grounded framework for scalable image generation, with implications for applications requiring high-quality, controllable synthesis.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We explore the use of Vector Quantized Variational AutoEncoder (VQ-VAE) models for large scale image generation. To this end, we scale and enhance the autoregressive priors used in VQ-VAE to generate synthetic samples of much higher coherence and fidelity than possible before. We use simple feed-forward encoder and decoder networks, making our model an attractive candidate for applications where the encoding and/or decoding speed is critical. Additionally, VQ-VAE requires sampling an autoregressive model only in the compressed latent space, which is an order of magnitude faster than sampling in the pixel space, especially for large images. We demonstrate that a multi-scale hierarchical organization of VQ-VAE, augmented with powerful priors over the latent codes, is able to generate samples with quality that rivals that of state of the art Generative Adversarial Networks on multifaceted datasets such as ImageNet, while not suffering from GAN’s known shortcomings such as mode collapse and lack of diversity.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose to bridge the gap between autoregressive (AR) language modeling and bidirectional context requirements in downstream tasks by leveraging unsupervised representation learning frameworks that transcend uni-directional density estimation. While AR models, trained to factorize likelihoods either forward or backward, excel in sequence prediction but fail to capture deep bidirectional dependencies, autoencoding (AE) pretraining—exemplified by BERT—achieves state-of-the-art performance by reconstructing masked tokens from corrupted inputs without explicit density modeling. Our work formalizes this contrast, demonstrating that AE-based pretraining, despite lacking direct likelihood estimation, yields superior representations for downstream tasks requiring bidirectional understanding. Experimental results across multiple benchmarks confirm that AE pretraining consistently outperforms AR models, particularly in tasks demanding contextual coherence beyond immediate neighbors. Theoretically, we show that AE’s reconstruction objective implicitly encodes bidirectional context through masked token recovery, enabling robust transfer learning. This underscores the broader significance of unsupervised pretraining paradigms that prioritize contextual reconstruction over autoregressive likelihood modeling for modern language understanding.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper addresses the limitation of autoregressive (AR) language models in capturing deep bidirectional context, which hinders their effectiveness for downstream language understanding tasks despite their success in unsupervised pretraining. We propose XLNet, a novel pretraining framework that leverages a permutation-based autoregressive modeling approach to jointly model forward and backward contexts. The model employs a masked two-stream attention mechanism, where the query stream is masked to prevent self-attention during token generation, while the content stream remains unmasked to preserve contextual information. Training proceeds by sampling a random permutation of token positions and applying autoregressive decoding in that order, enabling the model to learn bidirectional representations without explicit masking. Experimental results demonstrate XLNet’s superiority over BERT on multiple benchmark tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on GLUE and SQuAD benchmarks under identical training conditions. This advancement bridges the gap between autoregressive modeling and bidirectional context understanding, offering a more effective pretraining paradigm for natural language processing.
detailed_results.reference_summary: With the capability of modeling bidirectional contexts, denoising autoencoding based pretraining like BERT achieves better performance than pretraining approaches based on autoregressive language modeling. However, relying on corrupting the input with masks, BERT neglects dependency between the masked positions and suffers from a pretrain-finetune discrepancy. In light of these pros and cons, we propose XLNet, a generalized autoregressive pretraining method that (1) enables learning bidirectional contexts by maximizing the expected likelihood over all permutations of the factorization order and (2) overcomes the limitations of BERT thanks to its autoregressive formulation. Furthermore, XLNet integrates ideas from Transformer-XL, the state-of-the-art autoregressive model, into pretraining. Empirically, under comparable experiment settings, XLNet outperforms BERT on 20 tasks, often by a large margin, including question answering, natural language inference, sentiment analysis, and document ranking."

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a framework for unsupervised representation learning that leverages generative models to capture domain semantics directly from raw data, addressing the persistent gap between generative capability and semantic understanding in current models. Unlike self-supervised methods that rely on engineered pretext tasks—such as colorization or masked prediction—we train generative models to reconstruct full data distributions from partial or corrupted inputs, thereby forcing the model to learn meaningful latent representations through generative inversion. Our approach employs adversarial training with perceptual loss and latent space regularization to stabilize learning and enforce semantic coherence. Experiments on high-resolution image datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on downstream tasks including classification and clustering, while also recovering interpretable latent structures. Theoretically, we show that under mild assumptions, the learned representations are disentangled and invariant to nuisance factors. This work establishes generative modeling as a viable pathway to true semantic understanding, offering a foundation for unsupervised learning that transcends traditional self-supervision paradigms.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper addresses the persistent challenge of leveraging generative models to learn meaningful semantic representations from raw, unlabeled data, a goal hindered by the lack of direct supervision and the ambiguity of learned semantics. We propose a framework that integrates a variational autoencoder with a generative adversarial network, where an encoder maps data to latent variables, and a generator synthesizes data from these latents, jointly optimized under a novel loss function that combines adversarial and reconstruction terms. The training procedure explicitly encourages the encoder to learn disentangled, interpretable representations by constraining the generator’s ability to produce diverse outputs from fixed latent inputs. Experimental results on image datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms prior approaches, achieving state-of-the-art scores on both image quality metrics (FID, IS) and downstream classification accuracy, even when trained without explicit supervision. This work establishes a principled pathway to semantic understanding through generation, bridging the gap between generative modeling and representation learning.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Adversarially trained generative models (GANs) have recently achieved compelling image synthesis results. But despite early successes in using GANs for unsupervised representation learning, they have since been superseded by approaches based on self-supervision. In this work we show that progress in image generation quality translates to substantially improved representation learning performance. Our approach, BigBiGAN, builds upon the state-of-the-art BigGAN model, extending it to representation learning by adding an encoder and modifying the discriminator. We extensively evaluate the representation learning and generation capabilities of these BigBiGAN models, demonstrating that these generation-based models achieve the state of the art in unsupervised representation learning on ImageNet, as well as in unconditional image generation. Pretrained BigBiGAN models – including image generators and encoders – are available on TensorFlow Hub1.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a score-matching-based generative framework that circumvents the limitations of likelihood-based models and GANs by avoiding the need for normalized probability architectures or adversarial training. Unlike prior methods such as Infusion Training and Variational Walkback, which maximize evidence lower bounds and often yield blurry outputs, our approach directly optimizes a score-matching objective to train transition operators of a Markov chain using varying noise levels. This enables high-fidelity image generation without relying on surrogate losses or adversarial dynamics. Experimentally, our model achieves state-of-the-art inception scores on CIFAR-10 and FID scores comparable to SNGANs, demonstrating competitive quality with GANs while avoiding their training instability. The method generalizes beyond low-dimensional data, offering a theoretically grounded and practically effective alternative for generative modeling. Our work advances the field by establishing score matching as a viable, non-adversarial, and non-likelihood-based pathway to high-quality generative outputs, with broad applicability across machine learning domains.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a score-matching based generative model, NCSN, that directly optimizes a score function without requiring likelihood normalization or adversarial training, addressing the instability and evaluation challenges of GANs and the architectural constraints of likelihood-based models. Our framework trains a Markov chain transition operator via score matching, leveraging different noise levels during training to approximate the data distribution. Unlike prior methods such as Infusion Training and Variational Walkback, which maximize evidence lower bounds and produce blurry outputs, our approach yields sharper samples by avoiding likelihood estimation. Experimentally, NCSN achieves state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-10, with an Inception Score of 8.87 ± .12 and FID of 25.32 for unconditional generation, and an Inception Score of 9.22 and FID of 14.73 for conditional generation, outperforming leading models including SNGANs and EBM. This demonstrates that score matching can effectively compete with GANs and likelihood models, offering a more stable and theoretically grounded alternative for high-fidelity generative modeling.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We introduce a new generative model where samples are produced via Langevin dynamics using gradients of the data distribution estimated with score matching. Because gradients can be ill-defined and hard to estimate when the data resides on low-dimensional manifolds, we perturb the data with different levels of Gaussian noise, and jointly estimate the corresponding scores, i.e., the vector fields of gradients of the perturbed data distribution for all noise levels. For sampling, we propose an annealed Langevin dynamics where we use gradients corresponding to gradually decreasing noise levels as the sampling process gets closer to the data manifold. Our framework allows flexible model architectures, requires no sampling during training or the use of adversarial methods, and provides a learning objective that can be used for principled model comparisons. Our models produce samples comparable to GANs on MNIST, CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets, achieving a new state-of-the-art inception score of 8.87 on CIFAR-10. Additionally, we demonstrate that our models learn effective representations via image inpainting experiments.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose ViLBERT, a dual-stream vision-and-language model that jointly encodes visual and linguistic inputs through parallel transformer streams connected by co-attentional layers, addressing the critical gap between modality-specific representations and their semantic alignment. Building on BERT’s bidirectional attention mechanism, ViLBERT extends the architecture to handle multimodal data by enabling variable-depth processing per modality and sparse, cross-modal interactions via co-attention, preserving modality-specific features while learning their interdependencies. Evaluated on multiple vision-and-language benchmarks, ViLBERT consistently outperforms unified single-stream models and achieves state-of-the-art results, demonstrating that decoupled yet interactive streams yield superior performance compared to monolithic architectures. Theoretical and empirical analyses confirm that co-attention effectively bridges visual and linguistic representations, enabling models to capture nuanced correlations—such as linking “beagle” to dog breed images—without compromising modality-specific expressiveness. This work establishes a scalable, modular framework for multimodal transfer learning, offering a principled approach to aligning heterogeneous data sources in vision-and-language tasks.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose ViLBERT, a dual-stream vision-and-language model that jointly processes visual and textual inputs through parallel transformer encoders with novel co-attentional layers enabling sparse, modality-specific interaction. Addressing the gap between isolated visual and linguistic representations, ViLBERT overcomes the limitation of single-stream models by allowing variable depth per modality and explicit cross-modal alignment, thereby capturing the essential correspondence between visual concepts and their linguistic descriptions. Evaluated across six vision-and-language benchmarks including VQA, VCR, and RefCOCO+, ViLBERT achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming prior methods by significant margins—e.g., 70.55% accuracy on VQA test-dev and 84.90% R@1 on image retrieval. The architecture’s design enables efficient, scalable learning from large-scale pretraining, demonstrating that coordinated, bidirectional attention across modalities is critical for robust downstream task performance. This work establishes a foundational framework for multimodal representation learning, highlighting the necessity of structured cross-modal interaction over unified encoders for complex vision-and-language understanding.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present ViLBERT (short for Vision-and-Language BERT), a model for learning task-agnostic joint representations of image content and natural language. We extend the popular BERT architecture to a multi-modal two-stream model, processing both visual and textual inputs in separate streams that interact through co-attentional transformer layers. We pretrain our model through two proxy tasks on the large, automatically collected Conceptual Captions dataset and then transfer it to multiple established vision-and-language tasks – visual question answering, visual commonsense reasoning, referring expressions, and caption-based image retrieval – by making only minor additions to the base architecture. We observe significant improvements across tasks compared to existing task-specific models – achieving state-of-the-art on all four tasks. Our work represents a shift away from learning groundings between vision and language only as part of task training and towards treating visual grounding as a pretrainable and transferable capability.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a sparse attention mechanism for multi-head transformer architectures to address the interpretability and efficiency limitations of standard softmax-normalized attention, which assigns non-zero weights to all context tokens. Our method employs sparsemax as the normalizing transform, enabling exact zero weights for irrelevant tokens and promoting sparser, more interpretable attention distributions. We train the model end-to-end using standard transformer objectives while preserving the multi-head structure, allowing each head to specialize in distinct token relationships as suggested by recent interpretability studies. Experimental results on neural machine translation and language modeling tasks demonstrate that our sparse attention variant achieves competitive or superior performance compared to softmax-based baselines, while reducing computational overhead and improving attention sparsity. Theoretical analysis confirms that sparsemax preserves the expressive power of softmax while enhancing interpretability through zero-weighted token suppression. This work advances the design of attention mechanisms toward more interpretable, efficient, and task-adaptive architectures, with implications for scalable transformer models in NLP and beyond.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a novel approach to multi-head attention mechanisms in Transformers by replacing the standard softmax normalization with sparse, differentiable activation functions—specifically, entmax variants—to enable exact zero attention weights for irrelevant tokens, thereby enhancing both performance and interpretability. Addressing the limitation of softmax’s non-zero probability assignment for all context words, our method introduces 1.5-entmax and α-entmax, which adaptively learn sparsity parameters during training to better capture head-specific attention patterns. Experimental results across four translation tasks (DE→EN, JA→EN, RO→EN, EN→DE) demonstrate consistent or improved BLEU scores compared to softmax, with α-entmax achieving the highest performance (29.90 BLEU on DE→EN). Furthermore, training curves reveal that attention sparsity parameters α converge to stable, task-dependent values, indicating learned specialization of attention heads. This work advances the interpretability of Transformer architectures by aligning attention distributions with the observed functional specialization of attention heads, suggesting a path toward more efficient and interpretable models.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Attention mechanisms have become ubiquitous in NLP. Recent architectures, notably the Transformer, learn powerful context-aware word representations through layered, multi-headed attention. The multiple heads learn diverse types of word relationships. However, with standard softmax attention, all attention heads are dense, assigning a non-zero weight to all context words. In this work, we introduce the adaptively sparse Transformer, wherein attention heads have flexible, context-dependent sparsity patterns. This sparsity is accomplished by replacing softmax with α-entmax: a differentiable generalization of softmax that allows low-scoring words to receive precisely zero weight. Moreover, we derive a method to automatically learn the α parameter – which controls the shape and sparsity of α-entmax – allowing attention heads to choose between focused or spread-out behavior. Our adaptively sparse Transformer improves interpretability and head diversity when compared to softmax Transformers on machine translation datasets. Findings of the quantitative and qualitative analysis of our approach include that heads in different layers learn different sparsity preferences and tend to be more diverse in their attention distributions than softmax Transformers. Furthermore, at no cost in accuracy, sparsity in attention heads helps to uncover different head specializations.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a method that leverages full-network pre-training followed by task-specific fine-tuning to achieve state-of-the-art performance on the RACE reading comprehension benchmark, pushing machine accuracy from 44.1% to 89.4%—a 45.3% relative gain—demonstrating the critical role of high-quality pretrained language representations in complex NLP tasks. Building on recent advances in transformer architectures, our approach scales model capacity beyond prior limits by increasing hidden size, depth, and attention heads, while mitigating computational constraints through parameter sharing across layers. We train a large-scale transformer model on a diverse corpus, then fine-tune it end-to-end for RACE, achieving superior results compared to prior methods that capped at 1024 hidden units. Our experiments confirm that model size directly correlates with performance gains across multiple NLP tasks, validating the efficacy of scaling transformer architectures. This work underscores the transformative impact of pretrained representations on downstream comprehension tasks and provides a scalable framework for future language model development.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose ALBERT, a novel architecture for efficient pre-training of language representations that addresses the computational inefficiency of large transformer models like BERT by introducing parameter sharing across transformer layers. Unlike BERT, which scales parameter count linearly with model size, ALBERT shares parameters across all layers for the embedding and encoder components, drastically reducing parameter count while preserving performance. Our method trains the model end-to-end using full-network pre-training followed by task-specific fine-tuning, leveraging the same paradigm that has driven recent NLP breakthroughs. Experimental results demonstrate that ALBERT achieves state-of-the-art performance on the RACE reading comprehension benchmark, reaching 89.4% accuracy — a 45.3% improvement over prior work — while using significantly fewer parameters. Analysis of layer-wise representations confirms that parameter sharing preserves semantic coherence, as evidenced by stable cosine similarity and low L2 distance across layers. This work establishes a new paradigm for scaling language models efficiently, enabling high-performance representation learning with reduced computational cost.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Increasing model size when pretraining natural language representations often results in improved performance on downstream tasks. However, at some point further model increases become harder due to GPU/TPU memory limitations and longer training times. To address these problems, we present two parameter-reduction techniques to lower memory consumption and increase the training speed of BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Comprehensive empirical evidence shows that our proposed methods lead to models that scale much better compared to the original BERT. We also use a self-supervised loss that focuses on modeling inter-sentence coherence, and show it consistently helps downstream tasks with multi-sentence inputs. As a result, our best model establishes new state-of-the-art results on the GLUE, RACE, and SQuAD benchmarks while having fewer parameters compared to BERT-large. The code and the pretrained models are available at https://github.com/google-research/ALBERT."

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose BART, a denoising autoencoder architecture combining bidirectional and autoregressive Transformer components, designed as a sequence-to-sequence model for versatile pretraining across diverse downstream tasks. BART addresses the challenge of general-purpose language modeling by leveraging flexible noising functions applied to input text, followed by reconstruction via a standard Transformer-based encoder-decoder framework, which generalizes BERT’s bidirectional encoding and GPT’s autoregressive decoding. Pretraining optimizes the negative log likelihood of original documents, enabling robust adaptation to varied text transformations—including length-altering operations. Experimental results demonstrate BART’s efficacy, improving over a strong back-translation machine translation baseline by 1.1 BLEU on the WMT Romanian-English benchmark. Our implementation employs 12-layer encoders and decoders for large-scale models, and we validate performance across multiple noising schemes, suggesting substantial room for novel transformation designs. BART’s architecture and training paradigm offer a unified, extensible foundation for pretraining language models with broad applicability and theoretical flexibility.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose BART, a denoising autoencoder pre-trained via sequence-to-sequence modeling that unifies bidirectional and autoregressive Transformer architectures. Addressing the challenge of generalizing across diverse downstream tasks, BART learns to reconstruct original text from arbitrarily corrupted inputs, enabling flexible noising—including length alteration—during pre-training. The model employs a bidirectional encoder and a left-to-right autoregressive decoder, optimized by minimizing the negative log likelihood of the original document. Experimental results demonstrate BART’s efficacy across multiple benchmarks: it achieves state-of-the-art performance on SQuAD 1.1 (EM/F1: 88.8/94.6), MNLI (m/mm: 90.2/90.1), and MRPC (Acc: 90.4), outperforming BERT, UniLM, and XLNet. Furthermore, BART improves upon a strong back-translation machine translation baseline by 1.1 BLEU on the WMT Romanian-English task. These results confirm BART’s broad applicability and strong generalization capability, positioning it as a versatile pre-trained language model for diverse natural language understanding and generation tasks.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We present BART, a denoising autoencoder for pretraining sequence-to-sequence models. BART is trained by (1) corrupting text with an arbitrary noising function, and (2) learning a model to reconstruct the original text. It uses a standard Transformer-based neural machine translation architecture which, despite its simplicity, can be seen as generalizing BERT (due to the bidirectional encoder), GPT (with the left-to-right decoder), and many other more recent pretraining schemes. We evaluate a number of noising approaches, finding the best performance by both randomly shuffling the order of the original sentences and using a novel in-filling scheme, where spans of text are replaced with a single mask token. BART is particularly effective when fine tuned for text generation but also works well for comprehension tasks. It matches the performance of RoBERTa with comparable training resources on GLUE and SQuAD, achieves new state-of-the-art results on a range of abstractive dialogue, question answering, and summarization tasks, with gains of up to 6 ROUGE. BART also provides a 1.1 BLEU increase over a back-translation system for machine translation, with only target language pretraining. We also report ablation experiments that replicate other pretraining schemes within the BART framework, to better measure which factors most influence end-task performance.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose sandwich transformers, a novel architecture derived from empirical observations of randomly generated transformer models with fixed parameter counts. Through systematic random search on the WikiText-103 language modeling benchmark, we find that models prioritizing self-attention layers toward the bottom and feedforward layers toward the top consistently outperform the standard interleaved transformer. This insight motivates a reordering of sublayers that deviates from the conventional alternating pattern, instead stacking self-attention early and feedforward late. We train and evaluate this architecture against the Baevski and Auli (2019) baseline, demonstrating statistically significant performance gains. Our results suggest that architectural ordering—not merely layer composition—plays a critical role in transformer efficacy. This work provides a principled, data-driven approach to transformer design, offering a new architectural family that may inform future model optimization and reduce reliance on heuristic interleaving.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose sandwich transformers, a novel architecture family designed to optimize sublayer ordering within transformer models, motivated by empirical observations from random search experiments on the WikiText-103 benchmark. Our method systematically generates and trains random transformer models with fixed parameter counts, varying sublayer composition and sequence, to evaluate architectural efficacy beyond the standard interleaved pattern. Analysis reveals that models prioritizing self-attention layers toward the bottom and feedforward layers toward the top consistently outperform the baseline. Based on this insight, we introduce sandwich transformers, which enforce this non-interleaved, stratified sublayer arrangement. Experimental results demonstrate that sandwich transformers achieve significantly lower perplexity than the Baevski and Auli (2019) baseline, with the best-performing configuration achieving a PPL of 18.12, indicating superior language modeling capability through deliberate architectural design rather than random initialization.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Multilayer transformer networks consist of interleaved self-attention and feedforward sublayers. Could ordering the sublayers in a different pattern lead to better performance? We generate randomly ordered transformers and train them with the language modeling objective. We observe that some of these models are able to achieve better performance than the interleaved baseline, and that those successful variants tend to have more self-attention at the bottom and more feedforward sublayers at the top. We propose a new transformer pattern that adheres to this property, the sandwich transformer, and show that it improves perplexity on multiple word-level and character-level language modeling benchmarks, at no cost in parameters, memory, or training time. However, the sandwich reordering pattern does not guarantee performance gains across every task, as we demonstrate on machine translation models. Instead, we suggest that further exploration of task-specific sublayer reorderings is needed in order to unlock additional gains.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose dynamic convolution, a novel operator design that enhances representation capability in efficient CNNs with negligible additional FLOPs, addressing the critical performance degradation observed when computational budgets are severely constrained—e.g., MobileNetV3’s top-1 accuracy drops from 75.2% to 67.4% when FLOPs are reduced from 219M to 66M. Dynamic convolution replaces static convolution kernels with a set of K parallel kernels {W̃k, b̃k}, dynamically aggregating them per input via attention weights πk(x) to form W̃ = ∑k πk(x)W̃k, enabling adaptive feature extraction without increasing computational cost. This design is seamlessly integrated into existing architectures, such as MobileNetV2 and V3, by substituting standard convolutions. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent gains: dynamic convolution improves AP by 1.6%, 2.9%, and 2.2% on ResNet-18, MobileNetV2, and MobileNetV3-Small, respectively, in both image classification and human pose estimation. The method’s efficiency and compatibility make it a promising component for next-generation lightweight networks operating under extreme computational constraints.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose dynamic convolution, a novel operator design to enhance representation capability in efficient CNNs under extreme computational constraints, where existing models like MobileNetV3 suffer significant accuracy drops—e.g., from 75.2% to 67.4% when computational cost is reduced from 219M to 66M Multi-Adds. Dynamic convolution replaces static kernels with a set of K parallel kernels {W̃k, b̃k} aggregated dynamically per input x via W̃ = ∑k πk(x)W̃k, where πk(x) are input-dependent attention weights, enabling richer feature extraction without additional FLOPs. This operator is seamlessly integrated into existing architectures; replacing standard convolutions in MobileNetV2/V3 yields consistent performance gains: +1.6, +2.9, and +2.2 AP on ResNet-18, MobileNetV2, and MobileNetV3-Small, respectively. Experiments across varying computational budgets (×1.0 to ×0.35) confirm that dynamic convolution maintains or improves accuracy while preserving efficiency, demonstrating its robustness and scalability. This work establishes dynamic convolution as a critical component for next-generation efficient networks.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Light-weight convolutional neural networks (CNNs) suffer performance degradation as their low computational budgets constrain both the depth (number of convolution layers) and the width (number of channels) of CNNs, resulting in limited representation capability. To address this issue, we present Dynamic Convolution, a new design that increases model complexity without increasing the network depth or width. Instead of using a single convolution kernel per layer, dynamic convolution aggregates multiple parallel convolution kernels dynamically based upon their attentions, which are input dependent. Assembling multiple kernels is not only computationally efficient due to the small kernel size, but also has more representation power since these kernels are aggregated in a non-linear way via attention. By simply using dynamic convolution for the state-of-the-art architecture MobileNetV3-Small, the top-1 accuracy of ImageNet classification is boosted by 2.9% with only 4% additional FLOPs and 2.9 AP gain is achieved on COCO keypoint detection.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose MushroomRL, a flexible and comprehensive Python library for reinforcement learning designed to address the limitations of existing RL frameworks, which often lack extensibility, algorithm diversity, and ease of use. MushroomRL integrates standard scientific and machine learning libraries—including NumPy, SciPy, Scikit-Learn, PyTorch, and RL benchmarks such as OpenAI Gym and DeepMind Control Suite—enabling rapid prototyping and experimentation with minimal code. The framework supports a wide range of algorithms, with users able to define and run experiments in just a few lines of Python, eliminating the need for complex configuration. Empirical evaluations demonstrate its effectiveness in facilitating reproducible and scalable RL research, while its modular architecture allows seamless extension and customization. By unifying diverse tools and environments under a single, intuitive interface, MushroomRL lowers the barrier to entry for both novice and experienced researchers, accelerating innovation in RL without sacrificing flexibility or performance. Its design promotes transparency, reproducibility, and accessibility, positioning it as a foundational tool for future RL development.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose MushroomRL, a comprehensive and flexible Python library for Reinforcement Learning designed to address the limitations of existing frameworks, which often lack extensibility, algorithm diversity, and ease of use. The framework integrates standard Python libraries including NumPy, SciPy, Scikit-Learn, PyTorch, and major RL benchmarks such as OpenAI Gym and DeepMind Control Suite, enabling rapid experimentation with minimal code. MushroomRL offers a unified interface for implementing and testing a wide array of algorithms, categorized into value-based and policy-search methods, including both classical RL and modern Deep RL techniques like DQN, SAC, and PPO. Experimental results demonstrate the library’s efficacy in training and evaluating these algorithms, as shown by performance curves on benchmark tasks, confirming its ability to produce reliable and reproducible results. The framework’s design significantly reduces the barrier to entry for researchers, facilitating rapid prototyping and comparison of RL methods, thereby accelerating progress in the field.
detailed_results.reference_summary: MushroomRL is an open-source Python library developed to simplify the process of implementing and running Reinforcement Learning (RL) experiments. Compared to other available libraries, MushroomRL has been created with the purpose of providing a comprehensive and flexible framework to minimize the effort in implementing and testing novel RL methodologies. Indeed, the architecture of MushroomRL is built in such a way that every component of an RL problem is already provided, and most of the time users can only focus on the implementation of their own algorithms and experiments. The result is a library from which RL researchers can significantly benefit in the critical phase of the empirical analysis of their works. MushroomRL stable code, tutorials and documentation can be found at https://github.com/MushroomRL/mushroom-rl.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a novel framework for multi-domain image-to-image translation that addresses the scalability challenge of handling exponentially large domain spaces defined by combinatorial attribute sets. To overcome the inefficiency of training on all possible domain pairs, we introduce an Informative Sample Mining Network that identifies critical training samples via Adversarial Importance Weighting, integrating importance sampling into the GAN training pipeline. We further propose Multi-hop Sample Training to mitigate the hardness of mined samples, enabling stable and effective learning. Evaluated on facial attribute transfer, season transfer, and edge&photo transfer tasks, our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both attribute fidelity and content preservation, as measured by perceptual similarity and attribute accuracy metrics. Theoretical analysis confirms that our sampling strategy enhances gradient consistency and reduces mode collapse. This work advances scalable multi-domain translation by decoupling domain discovery from model training, offering a generalizable paradigm for high-dimensional visual mapping tasks.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose INIT, a novel framework for multi-domain image-to-image translation that addresses the challenge of efficiently handling vast numbers of visual domains defined by combinatorial attribute sets. Our method introduces Adversarial Importance Weighting, integrating importance sampling into the GAN training pipeline to dynamically mine informative samples, and Multi-hop Sample Training to mitigate the difficulty of training hard-to-learn samples. INIT employs a pre-trained embedding model to compute sample importance weights, which are then used to guide the generator and discriminator via a weighted adversarial loss. Experimental results across facial attribute transfer, season transfer, and edge&photo transfer demonstrate that INIT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both attribute transfer fidelity and content preservation. Our approach provides a theoretically grounded and practically effective solution for scalable multi-domain translation, offering a new paradigm for training robust generative models under complex domain conditions.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Abstract. The performance of multi-domain image-to-image translation has been significantly improved by recent progress in deep generative models. Existing approaches can use a unified model to achieve translations between all the visual domains. However, their outcomes are far from satisfying when there are large domain variations. In this paper, we reveal that improving the sample selection strategy is an effective solution. To select informative samples, we dynamically estimate sample importance during the training of Generative Adversarial Networks, presenting Informative Sample Mining Network. We theoretically analyze the relationship between the sample importance and the prediction of the global optimal discriminator. Then a practical importance estimation function for general conditions is derived. Furthermore, we propose a novel multi-stage sample training scheme to reduce sample hardness while preserving sample informativeness. Extensive experiments on a wide range of specific image-to-image translation tasks are conducted, and the results demonstrate our superiority over current state-of-the-art methods.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose LightGCN, a simplified graph neural network architecture tailored for recommendation systems, addressing the complexity and redundancy in existing GCN-based models. Unlike prior approaches that incorporate nonlinear transformations and multiple weight matrices, LightGCN eliminates these components, relying solely on linear aggregations of neighbor embeddings during message passing, thereby reducing computational overhead and enhancing training efficiency. The model leverages the user-item interaction graph, explicitly utilizing one-hop neighbor structures to refine user and item embeddings without requiring explicit feature engineering or interaction history augmentation. Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that LightGCN achieves state-of-the-art performance in both rating prediction and ranking tasks, often outperforming complex baselines while requiring fewer parameters and less training time. Theoretical analysis further supports its effectiveness by highlighting the sufficiency of linear propagation for capturing essential interaction patterns. LightGCN’s design offers a principled, minimalistic framework for graph-based recommendation, enabling scalable deployment and inspiring future simplifications in neural architectures for collaborative filtering.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose LightGCN, a simplified graph convolutional network architecture designed to enhance recommendation performance by eliminating unnecessary computational components found in prior GCN models. Addressing the challenge of model complexity in graph-based collaborative filtering, LightGCN removes nonlinear activation functions and collapses multiple weight matrices into a single one, thereby streamlining the message-passing mechanism while preserving the core graph structure of user-item interactions. The model leverages only the first-order neighbor aggregation, directly optimizing for recommendation accuracy without introducing additional parameters. Experimental results on benchmark datasets—Gowalla and Amazon-Book—demonstrate that LightGCN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models including NGCF and its variants, achieving significant gains in recall@20 and ndcg@20 metrics. Training curves further confirm its superior convergence stability and efficiency. This work establishes a theoretically grounded and practically effective paradigm for recommendation systems, highlighting that model simplification can yield substantial performance improvements when aligned with the task-specific structure of user-item graphs.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Graph Convolution Network (GCN) has become new state-of-the-art for collaborative filtering. Nevertheless, the reasons of its effectiveness for recommendation are not well understood. Existing work that adapts GCN to recommendation lacks thorough ablation analyses on GCN, which is originally designed for graph classification tasks and equipped with many neural network operations. However, we empirically find that the two most common designs in GCNs – feature transformation and nonlinear activation – contribute little to the performance of collaborative filtering. Even worse, including them adds to the difficulty of training and degrades recommendation performance.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose Feedback Memory, a straightforward yet effective mechanism to address key limitations of the Transformer architecture as an autoregressive model, particularly in sequential token prediction tasks such as language modeling. While Transformers’ parallelizable feedforward design enables efficient hardware utilization, it restricts access to higher-level, contextually enriched representations of past tokens during training, despite their availability at generation time. Feedback Memory mitigates this by enabling the model to retroactively access and integrate these higher-layer past representations during training, preserving the autoregressive sequential property without sacrificing parallelism. Our approach is implemented as a lightweight memory module that stores and retrieves past hidden states from higher transformer layers, dynamically updating the current representation with contextual feedback. Experiments demonstrate consistent performance gains across multiple language modeling benchmarks, with significant improvements in perplexity and generation quality. The method is theoretically grounded in the need to align training dynamics with generative behavior, offering a practical pathway to enhance autoregressive Transformer performance without architectural overhaul.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Feedback Memory, a straightforward modification to the Transformer architecture to address its limitations as an autoregressive model, particularly its inability to leverage sequential dependencies during training. The core innovation is a memory component that stores and retrieves higher-level, layer-wise representations of past tokens, enabling the model to access richer contextual information beyond the immediate past. This memory is updated sequentially during training and accessed via attention mechanisms during generation. Experimental results across diverse sequential tasks—including Copy, Reverse, Counting, Random Walk, and Algorithmic tasks—demonstrate significant performance gains: Feedback Memory achieves up to 40% relative improvement over the standard Transformer, with scores reaching 100% on the Random Walk task and 99.1% on Algorithmic tasks with three variables. These results confirm that explicitly incorporating past representations into the autoregressive process enhances model capability without altering the core Transformer structure. The approach offers a practical pathway to improve autoregressive models by bridging the gap between their efficient parallel training and their need to exploit sequential structure for better generation.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Transformers have been successfully applied to sequential, auto-regressive tasks despite being feedforward networks. Unlike recurrent neural networks, Transformers use attention to capture temporal relations while processing input tokens in parallel. While this parallelization makes them computationally efficient, it restricts the model from fully exploiting the sequential nature of the input. The representation at a given layer can only access representations from lower layers, rather than the higher level representations already available. In this work, we propose the Feedback Transformer architecture that exposes all previous representations to all future representations, meaning the lowest representation of the current timestep is formed from the highest-level abstract representation of the past. We demonstrate on a variety of benchmarks in language modeling, machine translation, and reinforcement learning that the increased representation capacity can create small, shallow models with much stronger performance than comparable Transformers.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose MobileBERT, a task-agnostic lightweight pre-trained model designed to enable generic fine-tuning across diverse downstream NLP tasks, addressing the critical gap in existing literature where task-specific distillation methods are costly and lack generalizability.
