Extensive experiments on ImageNet-1k, CIFAR, and Flowers-102 demonstrate ConvMLP’s competitive performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on ImageNet-1k with significantly fewer parameters and computational cost compared to ViT and ResMLP variants. The architecture’s efficiency and scalability across three variants—S, M, and L—highlight its practicality for real-world deployment, offering a compelling alternative to transformer-based models while preserving the expressive power of MLPs.
detailed_results.reference_summary: MLP-based architectures, which consist of a sequence of consecutive multi-layer perceptron blocks, have recently been found to reach comparable results to convolutional and transformer-based methods. However, most adopt spatial MLPs which take fixed dimension inputs, therefore making it difficult to apply them to downstream tasks, such as object detection and semantic segmentation. Moreover, single-stage designs further limit performance in other computer vision tasks and fully connected layers bear heavy computation. To tackle these problems, we propose ConvMLP: a hierarchical Convolutional MLP for visual recognition, which is a light-weight, stage-wise, co-design of convolution layers, and MLPs. In particular, ConvMLP-S achieves 76.8% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k with 9M parameters and 2.4 GMACs (15% and 19% of MLP-Mixer-B/16, respectively). Experiments on object detection and semantic segmentation further show that visual representation learned by ConvMLP can be seamlessly transferred and achieve competitive results with fewer parameters. Our code and pre-trained models are publicly available at https://github.com/SHI-Labs/Convolutional-MLPs

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose CPT, a pre-trained unbalanced Transformer model designed for joint natural language understanding and generation tasks in Chinese, addressing the gap in existing Chinese PTMs that are either task-specialized or computationally prohibitive. CPT integrates masked language modeling (MLM) and denoising autoencoding (DAE) tasks, with an individual decoder for MLM to mitigate DAE’s negative impact on NLU performance. Trained via multi-task pre-training, CPT achieves competitive results on both NLU and NLG benchmarks while maintaining parameter efficiency compared to large-scale counterparts like CPM and PanGu. Experimental results demonstrate CPT’s strong generalization across diverse downstream tasks, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art models in both domains with significantly reduced computational overhead. This work establishes a scalable, dual-purpose architecture that enables knowledge sharing between NLU and NLG, offering a practical solution for resource-constrained NLP applications in Chinese.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose CPT, a novel pre-trained unbalanced Transformer architecture designed to jointly optimize natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) tasks through a combined masked language modeling (MLM) and denoising autoencoding (DAE) pre-training framework. Addressing the gap in existing Chinese PTMs that specialize in either NLU or NLG by inheriting English model architectures, CPT employs a shared encoder (S-Enc) and two distinct decoders (U-Dec for MLM, G-Dec for NLG), enabling knowledge sharing while mitigating DAE’s negative impact on NLU. Experimental results demonstrate that CPT achieves competitive performance on both NLU and NLG benchmarks, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art Chinese models including BERT, RoBERTa, PanGu, and CPM. Crucially, CPT maintains this performance with a significantly smaller parameter count (121M) compared to large-scale counterparts (e.g., 207B for PanGu-α), offering superior parameter efficiency. This design enables CPT to serve as a versatile, lightweight backbone for diverse downstream tasks without requiring task-specific fine-tuning, thereby advancing the practical deployment of joint pre-training models in resource-constrained settings.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Abstract In this paper, we take the advantage of previous pre-trained models (PTMs) and propose a novel Chinese Pre-trained Unbalanced Transformer (CPT). Different from previous Chinese PTMs, CPT is designed to utilize the shared knowledge between natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) to boost the performance. CPT consists of three parts: a shared encoder, an understanding decoder, and a generation decoder. Two specific decoders with a shared encoder are pre-trained with masked language modeling (MLM) and denoising auto-encoding (DAE) tasks, respectively. With the partially shared architecture and multi-task pre-training, CPT can (1) learn specific knowledge of both NLU or NLG tasks with two decoders and (2) be fine-tuned flexibly that fully exploits the potential of the model. Moreover, the unbalanced Transformer saves the computational and storage cost, which makes CPT competitive and greatly accelerates the inference of text generation. Experimental results on a wide range of Chinese NLU and NLG tasks show the effectiveness of CPT*.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose Sym-NCO, a novel framework for neural combinatorial optimization that leverages symmetry as an inductive bias to enable efficient training of deep reinforcement learning models for NP-hard combinatorial problems. Unlike prior equivariant approaches such as EGNN, which fail to converge on coordinate-based optimization tasks due to insufficient modeling of input graph structure, Sym-NCO integrates symmetry-aware message passing into existing NCO architectures without requiring architectural modification. Our method exploits rotational symmetry inherent in Euclidean combinatorial optimization, reducing the effective training space and stabilizing learning. Empirical results demonstrate that Sym-NCO significantly outperforms EGNN, which underperforms and fails to converge on benchmark tasks, validating our hypothesis that equivariance alone is insufficient without structural alignment to problem-specific symmetries. Theoretical and experimental evidence confirms that symmetry-aware design enables scalable, near-optimal solutions in combinatorial domains, offering a principled pathway for training robust neural optimizers in complex, structured search spaces.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Sym-NCO, a novel framework for neural combinatorial optimization that explicitly leverages problem symmetry to enhance training efficiency and solution quality. Addressing the NP-hardness of combinatorial optimization, Sym-NCO introduces a symmetry-aware training procedure that constrains the search space via inductive bias, enabling more compact and effective deep reinforcement learning. Unlike prior equivariant models such as EGNN, which fail to converge on coordinate-based problems due to inadequate handling of input graph structure, Sym-NCO integrates seamlessly with existing powerful NCO architectures without requiring architectural modification. Experimental results on TSP and CVRP demonstrate that Sym-NCO achieves state-of-the-art performance: for TSP (N=100), it attains a 0.94% gap with only 3s runtime, and for CVRP (N=100), it achieves a 1.46% gap in 16s. These results confirm that symmetry exploitation is not only beneficial but essential for effective neural combinatorial optimization, offering a scalable and theoretically grounded approach to tackling complex discrete optimization problems.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based combinatorial optimization (CO) methods (i.e., DRL-NCO) have shown significant merit over the conventional CO solvers as DRL-NCO is capable of learning CO solvers less relying on problem-specific expert domain knowledge (heuristic method) and supervised labeled data (supervised learning method). This paper presents a novel training scheme, Sym-NCO, which is a regularizer-based training scheme that leverages universal symmetries in various CO problems and solutions. Leveraging symmetries such as rotational and reflectional invariance can greatly improve the generalization capability of DRL-NCO because it allows the learned solver to exploit the commonly shared symmetries in the same CO problem class. Our experimental results verify that our Sym-NCO greatly improves the performance of DRL-NCO methods in four CO tasks, including the traveling salesman problem (TSP), capacitated vehicle routing problem (CVRP), prize collecting TSP (PCTSP), and orienteering problem (OP), without utilizing problem-specific expert domain knowledge. Remarkably, Sym-NCO outperformed not only the existing DRL-NCO methods but also a competitive conventional solver, the iterative local search (ILS), in PCTSP at 240× faster speed. Our source code is available at https://github.com/alstn12088/Sym-NCO."

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a real-time object detector designed to operate efficiently across heterogeneous computing platforms—from mobile GPUs to edge and cloud GPUs—addressing the critical gap in existing architectures that prioritize either CPU or GPU optimization while neglecting cross-platform adaptability. Our framework integrates lightweight backbone modules inspired by MobileNet, ShuffleNet, and GhostNet for CPU-constrained edge devices, while leveraging ResNet, DarkNet, and DLA architectures optimized via CSPNet for GPU acceleration. The detector is trained end-to-end with a unified loss function that balances speed and accuracy across hardware domains, enabling seamless deployment without retraining. Experimental results demonstrate that our model achieves competitive mAP@0.5 metrics (e.g., 48.7% on COCO val) while maintaining inference speeds exceeding 100 FPS on mobile GPUs and 200 FPS on edge GPUs. This cross-platform compatibility significantly reduces deployment complexity and enhances real-time performance in applications ranging from autonomous driving to robotics, establishing a scalable, hardware-agnostic foundation for next-generation vision systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose YOLOv7, a real-time object detector designed to deliver high accuracy and speed across diverse hardware platforms, from mobile CPUs to cloud GPUs. Addressing the gap between edge-device efficiency and high-performance GPU inference, YOLOv7 introduces a novel architecture built upon the CSPNet strategy, leveraging lightweight backbones like MobileNet and ShuffleNet for CPU deployment, while scaling to ResNet and DarkNet for GPU acceleration. The model is trained end-to-end with a focus on optimizing both parameter count and FLOPs for efficiency. Experimental results on the MS COCO benchmark demonstrate that YOLOv7 achieves a 120% faster inference speed than YOLO, while maintaining or improving mAP across all scales. Compared to state-of-the-art detectors including YOLOX and YOLOR, YOLOv7 consistently outperforms them in both speed and accuracy, particularly on smaller input sizes. This dual-architecture design enables seamless deployment across heterogeneous computing environments, making YOLOv7 a versatile solution for real-time object detection in resource-constrained and high-performance settings alike.
detailed_results.reference_summary: YOLOv7 surpasses all known object detectors in both speed and accuracy in the range from 5 FPS to 160 FPS and has the highest accuracy 56.8% AP among all known real-time object detectors with 30 FPS or higher on GPU V100. YOLOv7-E6 object detector (56 FPS V100, 55.9% AP) outperforms both transformer-based detector SWIN-L Cascade-Mask R-CNN (9.2 FPS A100, 53.9% AP) by 509% in speed and 2% in accuracy, and convolutional-based detector ConvNeXt-XL Cascade-Mask R-CNN (8.6 FPS A100, 55.2% AP) by 551% in speed and 0.7% AP in accuracy, as well as YOLOv7 outperforms: YOLOR, YOLOX, Scaled-YOLOv4, YOLOv5, DETR, Deformable DETR, DINO-5scale-R50, ViT-Adapter-B and many other object detectors in speed and accuracy. Moreover, we train YOLOv7 only on MS COCO dataset from scratch without using any other datasets or pre-trained weights. Source code is released in https://github.com/WongKinYiu/yolov7.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose YOLOv6, a family of efficient object detectors designed to balance accuracy and speed across diverse industrial use cases, addressing the challenge of scalable, high-performance detection under resource constraints. The framework integrates refined label assignment, a tailored loss function, and adaptive data augmentation, selectively adopted from state-of-the-art anchor-free detectors to enhance performance. Architectures at multiple scales—YOLOv6-N, S, M, and L—are engineered with scale-specific backbones (single-path for small, multi-branch for large) to optimize speed-accuracy trade-offs. A novel quantization scheme, enhanced by RepOptimizer and channel-wise distillation, enables YOLOv6-S to achieve 43.3% COCO AP at 869 FPS (batch size 32), outperforming competitors including YOLOX-S and PPYOLOE-S. YOLOv6-M and L further demonstrate superior accuracy (49.5% and 52.3%) while maintaining competitive inference speeds. These results validate the effectiveness of each component, establishing YOLOv6 as a versatile, high-throughput detector suitable for real-time deployment.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose YOLOv6, a family of efficient object detectors designed to achieve superior accuracy-speed trade-offs across diverse deployment scenarios. Addressing the challenge of balancing performance and inference efficiency, YOLOv6 introduces a scalable architecture with distinct backbone designs—single-path for small models and multi-branch blocks for larger variants—to optimize speed and accuracy. Key innovations include refined label assignment, an enhanced loss function, and selective data augmentation techniques, all validated through systematic experimentation. The framework further incorporates a novel quantization scheme leveraging RepOptimizer and channel-wise distillation, enabling a quantized YOLOv6-S to achieve 43.3% COCO AP at 869 FPS (batch size 32). Across scales, YOLOv6-N, -S, -M, and -L consistently outperform mainstream detectors like YOLOv5, YOLOX, and PPYOLOE on both accuracy and throughput, with YOLOv6-L reaching 52.3% AP at 149 ms latency. These results demonstrate YOLOv6’s efficacy as a versatile, high-performance detection solution for industrial applications.
detailed_results.reference_summary: For years, YOLO series have been de facto industry-level standard for efficient object detection. The YOLO community has prospered overwhelmingly to enrich its use in a multitude of hardware platforms and abundant scenarios. In this technical report, we strive to push its limits to the next level, stepping forward with an unwavering mindset for industry application. Considering the diverse requirements for speed and accuracy in the real environment, we extensively examine the up-to-date object detection advancements either from industry or academy. Specifically, we heavily assimilate ideas from recent network design, training strategies, testing techniques, quantization and optimization methods. On top of this, we integrate our thoughts and practice to build a suite of deployment-

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a framework for improving the generalization of machine learning systems by moving beyond single-task, single-domain training toward robust multitask learning across diverse inputs and tasks. Current supervised systems, despite excelling on narrow, IID test sets, exhibit brittle behavior under distributional shifts and task variations, as evidenced by erratic performance in captioning, reading comprehension, and image classification. We argue that this brittleness stems from over-reliance on domain-specific datasets and task-specific training, and advocate for training on broad, heterogeneous benchmarks such as GLUE and decaNLP to foster transferable representations. Our method integrates multitask learning with cross-domain evaluation, enabling models to generalize across tasks without task-specific fine-tuning. Experimental results demonstrate consistent performance gains over single-task baselines across multiple NLP benchmarks, validating the framework’s effectiveness in promoting robustness. This approach offers a scalable path toward building more reliable, general-purpose AI systems capable of handling real-world variability.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose a multitask learning framework to enhance the generalization of large-scale language models across diverse downstream tasks, addressing the brittleness and narrow domain specialization of current systems trained on single-task, single-domain datasets. Our approach trains a unified model on a broad spectrum of tasks—including reading comprehension, translation, summarization, and question answering—using a shared architecture with varying parameter counts (117M to 1542M) and hidden dimensions (768 to 1600). We evaluate performance across these tasks using standard metrics such as F1, BLEU, ROUGE, and accuracy, demonstrating consistent and significant gains with model scaling. Results show that larger models achieve superior performance across all tasks, with the 1542M-parameter model outperforming smaller variants by substantial margins, particularly in complex tasks like open-domain question answering. This confirms that multitask training fosters robustness and generalization, offering a scalable pathway toward building more reliable and versatile AI systems beyond narrow expertise.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Natural language processing tasks, such as question answering, machine translation, reading comprehension, and summarization, are typically approached with supervised learning on task-specific datasets. We demonstrate that language models begin to learn these tasks without any explicit supervision when trained on a new dataset of millions of webpages called WebText. When conditioned on a document plus questions, the answers generated by the language model reach 55 F1 on the CoQA dataset - matching or exceeding the performance of 3 out of 4 baseline systems without using the 127,000+ training examples. The capacity of the language model is essential to the success of zero-shot task transfer and increasing it improves performance in a log-linear fashion across tasks. Our largest model, GPT-2, is a 1.5B parameter Transformer that achieves state of the art results on 7 out of 8 tested language modeling datasets in a zero-shot setting but still underfits WebText. Samples from the model reflect these improvements and contain coherent paragraphs of text. These findings suggest a promising path towards building language processing systems which learn to perform tasks from their naturally occurring demonstrations.

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose a generative pre-training framework to learn robust language representations from unlabeled text, addressing the critical challenge of reducing dependence on supervised data in NLP. Unlike prior methods that rely on word-level objectives, our approach leverages contextualized representations by training a transformer-based model to predict masked tokens in a self-supervised manner, enabling the model to capture richer linguistic patterns. The framework is designed to be transfer-agnostic, allowing downstream tasks to benefit from learned representations without architectural modification or auxiliary objectives. Extensive experiments across diverse NLP benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing unsupervised and supervised baselines, achieving state-of-the-art results on tasks such as question answering, natural language inference, and text classification. Theoretical analysis further confirms that the generative objective promotes coherent, semantically grounded representations. This work establishes generative pre-training as a powerful paradigm for transfer learning, offering a scalable, efficient alternative to manual annotation and paving the way for more accessible, high-performance NLP systems.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: We propose Generative Pre-Training (GPT) as a novel framework for learning robust language representations from unlabeled text, addressing the critical challenge of reducing dependence on supervised data in NLP. Unlike prior methods that rely on fixed objectives like language modeling or translation, GPT leverages autoregressive text generation to implicitly capture hierarchical linguistic structure. The model is trained end-to-end via a causal language modeling objective, where the Transformer architecture processes token sequences with masked multi-head self-attention and feed-forward layers, followed by layer normalization. We demonstrate that this approach yields state-of-the-art performance across diverse downstream tasks including natural language inference, question answering, and sentence similarity, evaluated on benchmark datasets such as SNLI, RACE, and STS Benchmark. Crucially, GPT achieves strong zero-shot transfer without task-specific architectural modifications or auxiliary objectives, establishing a new paradigm for unsupervised representation learning. This work underscores the efficacy of generative modeling for transfer learning and provides a scalable foundation for future NLP systems.
detailed_results.reference_summary: Natural language understanding comprises a wide range of diverse tasks such as textual entailment, question answering, semantic similarity assessment, and document classification. Although large unlabeled text corpora are abundant, labeled data for learning these specific tasks is scarce, making it challenging for discriminatively trained models to perform adequately. We demonstrate that large gains on these tasks can be realized by generative pre-training of a language model on a diverse corpus of unlabeled text, followed by discriminative fine-tuning on each specific task. In contrast to previous approaches, we make use of task-aware input transformations during fine-tuning to achieve effective transfer while requiring minimal changes to the model architecture. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a wide range of benchmarks for natural language understanding. Our general task-agnostic model outperforms discriminatively trained models that use architectures specifically crafted for each task, significantly improving upon the state of the art in 9 out of the 12 tasks studied. For instance, we achieve absolute improvements of 8.9% on commonsense reasoning (Stories Cloze Test), 5.7% on question answering (RACE), and 1.5% on textual entailment (MultiNLI).

detailed_results.summary_text_only: We propose adversarial nets as a novel framework for training deep generative models that circumvents the limitations of traditional generative approaches reliant on Markov chains and feedback loops. Unlike generative stochastic networks or denoising auto-encoders, adversarial nets enable backpropagation through the generator without requiring iterative sampling, thereby fully leveraging the gradient stability of piecewise linear units while avoiding unbounded activation issues. The framework trains a generator to produce samples indistinguishable from real data, competing against a discriminator via minimax optimization, enabling direct gradient flow through the generative network. Experimental results demonstrate superior sample quality and training stability compared to prior generative methods, particularly in high-dimensional domains. Theoretically, the approach provides a tractable path to approximate complex probability distributions without intractable likelihood computations. This work bridges the gap between discriminative successes in deep learning and generative modeling, offering a scalable, theoretically grounded method for unsupervised representation learning and data generation with broad implications for AI systems requiring synthetic data or probabilistic modeling.
detailed_results.summary_multimodal: This paper introduces adversarial nets as a novel framework for training deep generative models, addressing the challenge of leveraging piecewise linear units—whose gradients are well-behaved for backpropagation but problematic in feedback loops—within generative architectures. Unlike generative stochastic networks (GSNs) that require Markov chain sampling, adversarial nets eliminate feedback loops during generation, enabling direct backpropagation through the entire model. The core method involves training a discriminator to distinguish real data from generated samples, driving the generator to produce increasingly realistic outputs. Experimental results on MNIST and TFD datasets demonstrate superior performance: adversarial nets achieve 225 ± 2 and 2057 ± 2 log-likelihoods respectively, outperforming DBNs, stacked CAEs, and Deep GSNs. This advancement enables scalable, efficient training of deep generative models that can capture complex data distributions, bridging the gap between discriminative successes and generative modeling, and paving the way for more expressive probabilistic representations in AI.
detailed_results.reference_summary: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, in which we simultaneously train two models: a generative model G that captures the data distribution, and a discriminative model D that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than G. The training procedure for G is to maximize the probability of D making a mistake. This framework corresponds to a minimax two-player game. In the space of arbitrary functions G and D, a unique solution exists, with G recovering the training data distribution and D equal to ½ everywhere. In the case where G and D are defined by multilayer perceptrons, the entire system can be trained with backpropagation. There is no need for any Markov chains or unrolled approximate inference networks during either training or generation of samples. Experiments demonstrate the potential of the framework through qualitative and quantitative evaluation of the generated samples.
